title: Donna Lynn Castellano v Mavis Clark and Anor (CL 158 of 2019) [2026] TCASC 20 (27 March 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF
THE TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
Action No. CL158/19
BETWEEN:
DONNA LYNN CASTELLANO PLAINTIFF
-AND-
(1) MAVIS LOUISE ALLISON CLARK
(2) MAVIS LOUISE ALLISON CLARK
(in her capacity as administrator of the Estate
of the late ANDREW JAMES CLARK) DEFENDANTS
CORAM: AGYEMANG CJ
APPEARANCES:
MR. ARIEL MISICK KC, WITH HIM MS DEBORAH JOHN-WOODRUFFE FOR THE
PLAINTIFF
MR. CONRAD GRIFFITHS KC, WITH HIM MR DEVONTE SMITH FOR THE
DEFENDANTS
Judgment Handed Down on 27 March 2026
1

JUDGMENT
1. By her Amended Writ of Summons and Re-Amended Statement of Claim, the Plaintiff,
seeks the following reliefs:
(1) A Declaration that the Plaintiff and the Deceased were in a common law relationship under the
law of Manitoba, Canada between August 1998 and 1 March 2016.
(2) A Declaration that the Will was valid and effectual as of the date of its execution, and the
Deceased died testate.
(3) A Declaration that the dispositive provisions of the Will remain in effect and were not revoked
by the Separation Agreement or otherwise.
(4) A Declaration that the Plaintiff is entitled to the beneficial interest in the Deceased’s Estate.
(4A) An order that the Letters of Administration be revoked.
(5) An order requiring the Defendant to transfer all assets in the Deceased’s Estate to the Plaintiff.
(6) An order requiring the Defendant to account to the Plaintiff for all dealings and transactions
made by her, or on her behalf in the Deceased’s Estate.
(7) Payment of all proceeds of such dealings and transactions on the taking of the account.
(8) All other necessary and consequential directions, accounts, and inquiries.
(9) An injunction restraining the 2nd Defendant from disposing, charging, otherwise dealing with
any of the assets of the Deceased’s Estate whether inside or outside the jurisdiction of this court
other than for the purpose of administering, preserving, securing, protecting and realizing the value
of such assets.
(10) Equitable compensation for any loss suffered by the Plaintiff as a result of any unlawful
dealings or dissipation of the Deceased’s Estate.
(11) Compound interest on all sums found due to be due and owing in equity.
(12) Interest pursuant to section 19 of the Civil Procedure [Act].
(13) Costs of suit.
2

The Parties
1. The Plaintiff brings this suit, as executrix and beneficiary of the Will of Andrew Clark
which was executed on 15 April 2005. The Deceased died in Providenciales, Turks and
Caicos Islands (TCI), on 9 August 2018.
The Plaintiff brings the suit against the mother of Andrew: Mavis Clark, in her personal
capacity as first Defendant, and as second Defendant in her capacity as administratrix of
Andrew’s estate by virtue of Letters of Administration granted to her by this Court in 2019.
For easier reference, the Plaintiff is referred to also as Donna, and the Defendant is referred
to also as Mavis; the Deceased is referred to as Andrew.
Background Facts
2. This is a summary of the facts of this case:
Andrew was a Canadian citizen, who lived in the Canadian province of Manitoba before
he became a resident of TCI. In 1998, while he lived in Manitoba, he commenced a
relationship with the Plaintiff (Donna).
Andrew and Donna (the couple) first met as work colleagues in a firm called Nygard
International. Later they both moved to a firm called SHL Systemhouse Inc. They were
both divorced persons: Andrew from his marriage to one Richelle, and Donna, from her
seventeen-year marriage to marriage to a Mr. Castellano.
3. In August 1998, Andrew moved into Donna’s home at 725 Pritchard Farm Road, East St
Paul in Manitoba where she lived with her two children: Christopher Castellano (Chris)
and Tara Rae Castellano (Tara). Chris and Tara were fourteen years and nine years old
respectively at the time. The couple lived with the two children and a family dog: Bozley,
a Shih Tzu breed, until Chris moved out of the house and returned to live again for one
year in 2009 with his son (Cobain) born in January 2008. Tara left the home to live on her
own in 2009.
4. In 2000, Andrew, an internet specialist, obtained employment with Microsoft Inc in
Washington DC, USA, and moved to Virginia to take up that employment. In Virginia, he
rented accommodation at Lotus Lane, Centreville, in his name, and purchased a black
Celica motor vehicle which was later sold. Later, he purchased an Avalanche truck, as well
as a motorcycle, also in his name.
3

5. In 2002, Donna commenced work at the Workmen’s Compensation Board in Manitoba.
She joined its pension scheme in that year. She also became a client of a Ms. Faye Sierhus,
an independent financial consultant contracted to Manulife as a dealer/broker (the Financial
Advisor). In December of that year, she introduced Andrew to the Financial Advisor.
Andrew, however, only became a client of the Financial Advisor after he left Microsoft.
This was in accordance with her Manulife licensing which did not permit her to have a
USA client.
6. In 2004, Andrew returned to Manitoba from Virginia, having been transferred by his
employers to their Winnipeg office. The Deceased moved back into 725 Pritchard Farm
Road.
7. In 2005, Andrew and Donna made Wills, it appears, at the suggestion (or direction) of the
Financial Advisor. In Andrew’s Will, he appointed Donna executrix and beneficiary of his
Will; he also appointed in the alternative, his own father Robert (Bob) Clark as an executor,
if Donna’s appointment failed. Andrew also appointed Donna his Attorney, and at an
undisclosed date, nominated her as beneficiary of his life insurance policy.
8. In or about 2006, the Plaintiff and the Deceased commenced work on the basement of 725
Pritchard Farm Road house. According to Donna it was to give Tara who was then fifteen
years old, some privacy. They constructed a bedroom, a recreational room, a washroom
and storage facility. Tara was the first occupant of the basement where she had her
bedroom, although the recreational room was also used by Chris to entertain his friends.
9. Andrew also, in 2006, purchased property at River’s Drive, Manitoba in his sole name and
constructed a house on it. He sold it the next year.
10. In 2008, Andrew applied to become a client of the Financial Advisor. He declared the
Plaintiff as his common-law spouse in his formal application. Their accounts were linked
(referred to as being ‘householded’) which meant that their quarterly statements went to
the same house address. Andrew made the Plaintiff his beneficiary. This nomination did
not change although curiously, Andrew, later described Donna as his ‘friend’ in an
application for Manulife Securities Tax-Free Savings Account.
11. The couple kept a joint account at Cambrian Credit Union from an undisclosed date, until
2011 when the Plaintiff’s name was removed from the account, and the account continued
in Andrew’s sole name.
4

12. Some time in 2009, Andrew was dismissed from his work at Microsoft (unfairly, it seems,
for he challenged his dismissal successfully and secured a modest severance award). He
later (it is unclear how many months thereafter), commenced work at Hewlett Packard. In
or about 2009, Tara moved out of the house, taking her bed and room furnishing in the
basement with her.
13. In 2010 or about this time, with the help of the Financial Advisor who introduced him to
two accountants and her own personal banker to facilitate bridge financing for a real estate
business, Andrew purchased a house at 612 Hetherington Avenue, Fort Rouge, Manitoba,
in 2010, and renovated it. He sold it the after one year at a profit of $20,000.
The Deceased did not live in either of the properties: River’s Drive or 612 Hetherington
Avenue.
14. In 2011, the Deceased, left his work at Hewlett Packard. Tara having moved out of the
basement where she had her bedroom, Andrew converted it into an office and commenced
its use as his office. Andrew purchased a business Renderotica in his sole name. It was a
three-dimensional (3D) Graphics online store which sold adult 3D Art content. In that same
year, the Plaintiff put Andrew on her medical benefits, but her name was removed from the
couple’s joint account at the Cambrian Credit Union. In 2012, Andrew transferred the assets
of Renderotica to a business he incorporated as CG Bytes, a business that continued to
carry on the business of a 3D Art online store, but without the adult content.
15. In 2012, Andrew, seeking a route to reducing his tax liability in Canada, met with KPMG,
specialist accountants and tax advisors. They advised Andrew that he would not pay tax in
Canada if he was not resident in any year, and to fulfil this non-residency requirement, he
had to be out of Canada for a period of one hundred and eighty-three days, in any particular
year.
16. Andrew had discussions with the Financial Advisor on possible relocation, to enable him
avoid paying tax on his income. He informed the Financial Advisor that he had to be out
of Canada by 4 July 2013, to fulfil the one hundred and eighty-three days requirement for
non-resident status. Andrew’s preference for the relocation, was to a country where he
would avoid paying tax on his global income. He settled on TCI.
17. In his move to the TCI to fulfil the non-residency requirement, the Financial Advisor gave
him a reference to the Belize Bank to facilitate his relocation.
5

18. In April 2012, the Plaintiff and Andrew travelled to TCI, and returned to Canada after a
week’s visit during which they explored options on the Island until they decided to first
rent, and then learn a bit about the Island before any possible purchase of real estate.
Andrew communicated with a realtor: Mr. Peter Crawford Smith, that he and the Plaintiff
- whom he described as his wife - were seeking rental property to occupy for six months in
the year.
19. In April 2013, Andrew negotiated the rental of a one-bedroom apartment at the Atrium with
a Mr. Alain Wattiez, commencing 1 July 2013, for one year. The rental was in his sole
name.
20. That visit for one week was the Plaintiff’s only visit to TCI in 2013. Andrew however
returned to TCI for another two-week stay, and later in August 2013, Tara went to visit with
him at the Atrium for a month (18 August to 17 September 2013). It was a visit planned
with the Plaintiff, but which she could not fulfil due to work commitments.
21. In September 2013, Andrew applied for a residence permit. He remained in TCI through
Christmas, but left in May 2014 for Manitoba, where he stayed at 725 Pritchard Farm Road
for the summer months. He returned to TCI from Manitoba with Donna. Andrew’s stay in
TCI in 2014 was in total, one hundred and twenty-eight days. the remainder of the time, he
spent in Manitoba at 725 Pritchard Farm Road. Donna was unhappy with the way he stayed
in the basement in the four months he was home, and wrote to him about it.
22. On that October 2014 visit, he commenced discussions with Mr. Alain Wattiez for the lease
of a two-bedroom apartment at The Atrium from 1 October 2014. However, he did not
follow through with that quest, but completed the purchase of a one-bedroom condominium
at The Alexandra Resort in February 2015 in his sole name. The reasons for his choice of
a condominium at The Alexandra over The Atrium, were communicated in an email to Mr.
Wattiez. Andrew informed Mr. Wattiez that The Alexandra condominium was sold at a
‘rock bottom’ price, and also, that The Alexandra had the advantage of being on the beach,
and would be a lure for tourists. This was because, as he had previously intimated to Mr.
Wattiez at the Atrium, he would not always be in TCI, and “the unit would stand vacant
for 4-5 months each year”.
23. In April 2015, the Plaintiff and the Deceased returned to Providenciales, and having
discussed the refurnishing of the condominium at Alexandra, went to a shop referred to as
6

Design Studio where they discussed blinds, and other furnishings to be purchased for the
project. In emails to the Design Studio as to Mr. Wattiez, Andrew referred to the Plaintiff
as his wife, and indicated that they were involved in the property acquisition together.
24. The Plaintiff returned to Canada after an eleven-day stay, but Andrew (and the dog Bozley)
remained in Providenciales.
25. In May 2015, Andrew commenced the process of setting up a local bank account with First
Caribbean Bank in TCI (CIBC), declaring in to the bank, that he had moved full time to
TCI.
26. In August 2015, Andrew, Donna and Cobain, paid a visit to Tara in Montreal. Andrew
returned to TCI in September, and Donna visited with Andrew in TCI from the end of
October, into November 2015 for seven days.
27. In December 2015, Andrew returned to the 725 Pritchard Farm Road Manitoba, for
Christmas, until 6 January 2016. He was present at an early birthday celebration for Cobain
on 4 January 2016.
28. On 13 January 2016, both Andrew and the Plaintiff participated in The Alexandra’s annual
general meeting: Andrew did so in person but Donna participated virtually from Canada.
29. Donna, having in November 2015, begun to discuss her retirement plans with their
Financial Advisor, received advice to get her pension numbers run for the purpose. She
therefore in January 2016, commenced the process with the Workmen’s Compensation
Board (WCB), working with the company AON.
30. It was during this process, that Donna discovered that she had not declared Andrew her
common-law partner even though they qualified as such and she had placed him on her
health benefits. To correct this, she received advice from Pensions Department of the
Workmen’s Compensation Board (WCB to fill out some forms. This, she communicated to
Andrew.
31. On 27 January 2016, Andrew signed the WCB Marriage/Common-law Relationship
Breakdown Form sent to him by Donna. He stated therein that his last date of cohabitation
with Donna was 1 July 2013. The form was however not submitted. The Plaintiff took issue
with WCB over the word ‘cohabitation’ which appeared on the form to be checked off,
rather than the word ‘separation’. The form was officially changed, and ‘cohabitation’ was
replaced with ‘separation’. Donna following this, placed Andrew on her pension plan.
7

32. On 24 February 2016, Donna paid an unscheduled (surprise) visit to Andrew at
Providenciales. The ‘surprise’ turned out to be an unpleasant one, for the Plaintiff who
entered the condominium with her key, found a situation, which led to a revelation and
confession, that Andrew had had intimate relations with another woman.
33. The Plaintiff’s reaction to this was to leave for Canada the next day.
34. On 10 March 2016, Donna wrote a plaintive email to Andrew, titled ‘Reflection.pdf’ in
which she lamented that they had been so busy: “everything points to not having time for
just us…and nothing else’ and that they had allowed ‘separate lives to happen without
stopping to evaluate [them as a couple]’. Among the matters she spoke of, was that she
had supported and contributed to the move to TCI with an understanding that she ‘was in
the equation’, and expressed the hope that they could consider some time to give a ‘fair
and final evaluation’. It was an apparent last-ditch attempt to see if the relationship should
be salvaged or abandoned.
35. Andrew returned to Canada for three days: from 7-10 April 2016. On 10 April 2016, at a
birthday celebration for Pepper (Chris’ daughter), they informed Chris about their decision
to separate. Tara was also informed of it separately by both Andrew and Donna on
telephone.
36. It was at this time that the process of determining pension numbers for Donna’s retirement
which was begun in January 2016 and involved the signing of documents regarding their
common-law relationship, was transformed into a process for their separation.
37. The parties had lawyers who were at this time talking with each other: Terri Mestdagh was
in touch with the Plaintiff’s lawyer Alex on behalf of Andrew. The discussions were for the
most part not acrimonious, their sticking point being the date of end of cohabitation, and/or
separation, for while the Plaintiff requested that they ought to use 1 March 2016 as their
separation date, Andrew was adamant that it was 1 July 2013, for he maintained that it was
when his principal residence changed to TCI.
38. During the back-and-forth negotiation, Donna, on 6 July 2016, went online to a website:
lawdepot, pulled out a template for a Separation Agreement, and sent it to Andrew, asking
that they consider using it or continue on their previous course regarding the pension
waiver and homestead release.
8

39. The separating couple, thereafter started negotiating on their separation using that template.
Finally, after their back-and-forth negotiations, each presented the other with the
Agreement they were willing to sign. At that point, the only issue that did not appear to
have been clearly addressed was custody of the family dog: Bozley.
40. Andrew and Donna signed the Separation Agreement dated 31 August 2016. To witness
his signing, Andrew engaged an Attorney Tarrance De La Ronde for that limited purpose,
as well as for the transmission of the relevant documents, being: The Release of Homestead
Rights; the WCB Form; the Workers Compensation Board of Manitoba Retirement Plan;
and the Pension Benefits Spousal / Common Law Partners Agreement.
41. The Separation Agreement which recited that the parties thereto had commenced living
‘separate and apart’ from each other on 1 March 2016, also contained four specific releases
and a general release. Among the specific releases was Clause 10, which was under the
head: “Estate and Testamentary Disposition”. It read: “The Parties renounce all rights
each might have in, or to the administration of the other’s estate whether under any law of
the Province of Manitoba, or any Province or Territory of Canada, or any country in which
any part of the estate of the other may be situated and further waive and release the other
from any and all rights of every kind, nature, and description that each may acquire as a
spouse or a surviving spouse in the property, assets, or estate of the other.”
42. After the signing of the Separation Agreement, Donna travelled on 24 September 2016, to
TCI, to retrieve the dog Bozley while Andrew was on holiday in Europe with his then
girlfriend Rebecca Strohl (who became his fiancée). The ill-advised effort was viewed as
an attempt to kidnap the dog, and resulted in a complaint of assault by the dog-sitter against
Donna to the police. The incident appeared to be the last incident relating to the end of
Andrew and Donna’s relationship.
43. Two years later, on 9 August 2018, the Rebecca Strohl, Andrew’s fiancée, found him dead
in his closet in his condominium at The Alexandra.
44. The parties herein (Donna and Mavis), following the news of the Andrew’s demise, were
in amicable contact. In fact, the Plaintiff directed the Defendant to the lawyer who had
custody of Andrew’s 2005 Will. She also offered advice to Mavis on what she would need
to know for her trip to the TCI.
9

45. The amity between the parties did not last long however, and they found themselves on
opposite sides with regard to the estate of Andrew. First, Mavis on 8 August 2018, filed a
pre-emptive process in the Court of Queen’s Bench at Manitoba (the Manitoba Court),
seeking to prevent the Plaintiff from being granted probate of Andrew’s Will which named
the Plaintiff both executrix and beneficiary. Mavis’ application was on the ground that the
Separation Agreement the parties signed on 31 August 2016, had revoked the Will.
46. The Plaintiff subsequently, on 6 December 2018, petitioned the court for a grant of probate
of the contested Will.
47. While this process was pending, the Defendant applied for a grant of Letters of
Administration of the estate of the Deceased in TCI. She included a notice of her petition
in the papers she filed before the Manitoba Court. The Plaintiff did not lodge a caveat
against the grant sought, and the Letters of Administration were granted to the Defendant.
48. The Manitoba Court which had been called upon to declare the Will valid, having heard
the parties, determined that the issues relating to Andrew’s estate should be heard and
determined in the TCI as it was the forum conveniens. The decision was based upon the
court’s finding, that Andrew’s domicile was TCI at the time of his death. Donna’s appeal
against this decision was unsuccessful.
The Evidence
The Plaintiff’s Case
49. In these proceedings, commenced by Donna as Plaintiff in 2019, she seeks inter alia, a
declaration that Andrew’s Will is valid. She also seeks to enforce her entitlement to the
bequests and devises Andrew made to her under the Will. As a consequential relief, she
seeks a revocation of the Letters of Administration granted to Mavis by this Court on the
unestablished premise that Andrew died intestate.
50. The Plaintiff asserts as her case, a common-law relationship she had with Andrew which
ended on 1 March 2016, less than three years before Andrew’s death in July 2018. Thus
the Will executed by Andrew in 2005, remains valid, not revoked by the Separation
Agreement they executed on 31 August 2016, nor are the bequests to her, revoked by the
operation of s. 18(4)(e) of the Wills Act, which revokes such bequests after separation of
common-law partners three years before the death of the testator.
10

Common-law Relationship
Conjugal Relationship
51. In proof of her case, Donna has adduced evidence regarding the common law relationship
she alleges, which she says, commenced from the time Andrew moved into her house at
725 Pritchard Farm Road, in August 1998, until they separated on 1 March 2016.
52. Among the things she points to as evidence of this, was the conjugal relationship they
carried on, sharing the same bed in their family home at 725 Pritchard Farm Road,
presenting socially as a couple and a family together with their children, making financial
decisions and sharing finances, and sharing in each other’s property “under the mutual
understanding that [they] both own[ed] the condo and the Pritchard Farm house as
common-law partners."
53. In summary, Donna’s evidence in this regard, was, that she and Andrew exchanged vows
in a private ceremony after they got engaged but never got married, because Andrew was
an atheist. Thereafter, they lived in a common-law union at 725 Pritchard Farm Road
which was their primary residence and family home. There they lived in a conjugal
relationship which was unbroken despite Andrew’s sojourn in Virginia, USA (as they
travelled back and forth to be with each other, and for holidays), until Andrew returned to
the family home in 2004 on his transfer from Washington DC to Microsoft’s Manitoba
office. At no time had Andrew lived in the family home as a tenant, for on the contrary, he
acquired homestead rights as a common-law partner in that family home which rights he
released as part of their separation arrangements. Their conjugal relationship she averred
continued even when Andrew moved to TCI, for despite seeking residency in TCI, he
continued to spend time in Manitoba (five or six months in the year), and was present for
holidays and family events, and the Plaintiff on occasion, came to TCI to be with him. They
also maintained constant contact through Skype mostly, but sometimes by email, and they
travelled together to Great Britain, to Las Vegas, to visit Tara and once, to visit with
Andrew’s uncles. They also both continued to maintain their family home, contributing to
it financially. They accepted that they could live as many couples did in separate places,
due to the nature of their work. Even so, and although she had expressed misgivings on
settling at TCI due to rising crime and “corruption”, she had on the advice of their Financial
11

Advisor, commenced the process of getting her pension numbers determined, for
retirement, with a view to relocating to TCI to be with Andrew in a continued conjugal
relationship. They maintained an attitude of fidelity towards each other, until the
Deceased’s revealed infidelity on 24 February 2016, led to their relationship broke down
and eventual separation. Donna was confronted in cross-examination with an email she
wrote to Andrew complaining that he stayed in the basement throughout hi four months
stay at home. Her explanation was that although they did a few things together socially as
a family and been travelled to Houston for a big family graduation Andrew was so focused
on his business that he spent a lot of time in the basement - ‘a good portion of the time.’
She claimed that the email was n expression of her frustration with how he had conducted
himself when he should have been present with the family.
Children
54. Donna also asserted that although her relationship with Andrew did not produce a child,
(their attempt to have one having failed through a miscarriage in 1999), Andrew was a
father to the children of the household: Chris and Tara, and grandfather to Cobain and
Pepper, and that they participated in social activities and holidays, as a family. Although
she admitted that in 2013, Chris and Tara were both adults and had left home to live on
their own, she averred that Andrew was a grandfather who played a part in the upbringing
of Chris’ children: Cobain and Pepper.
Society and Family
55. Donna maintained that she and Andrew presented themselves to friends, family, and even
third parties, as a couple, and together with their children, as a family. It was her evidence
in this regard, that she went with Andrew and his father Robert (Bob) Clark to visit his
uncles Jim and John in British Columbia and maternal grandmothers. She averred that their
relationship was acknowledged by both their extended families, such that in Bob Clark’s
funeral programme, she was named along with Chris, Tara and Cobain as Andrew’s family;
Andrew was also included in the funeral programme of her own step-father as part of her
family. They presented as a couple in a conjugal relationship to third parties including their
neighbour, their financial Advisor with whom they had both a personal and professional
12

relationship. In TCI, Andrew held her out as his wife, and Tara as his daughter to third
parties with whom he communicated in their search for real estate.
Financial Decisions and Property Ownership
56. The Plaintiff averred that she and Andrew shared their resources, and contributed $1000
towards necessities in their home and that they made financial decisions together. She
however, accepted in cross-examination that Andrew’s contribution was what she
described in her email to him, as ‘rent’ of $700. It was her evidence that they had kept a
joint account at the Cambrian Credit Union which she opted out of in 2011 to mitigate their
risk as a family, at a time when Andrew’s business (which became CG Bytes) was new, and
there was a risk of having a lien on their home or account. She alleged that despite this,
they continued to make joint financial decisions.
57. According to Donna, they conducted a business venture together, pointing to the purchase
and sale (flipping) of two houses: 612 Hetherington Avenue and 156 Rivers Drive, which
although both held, and sold by Andrew in his sole name she was involved in. They also
understood that as common-law partners under Manitoba law, they had a share in each
other’s properties both in Manitoba (the family home in her name) and TCI ( the rental of
the apartment at The Atrium and the purchase of the condominium at The Alexandra in
Andrew’s name). Thus, Andrew introduced her as his wife to Mr. Peter Crawford Smith, a
realtor; Mr. Allan Whates and his wife Olga, landlords at the Atrium; Mr. Wattiez also of
the Atrium, and subsequently with Debbie and Heidi at Design Studio at TCI. On that basis,
was she included in all the real estate decisions and discussions with the said persons.
While Donna acknowledged (during cross-examination) that she had not provided funds
for the purchase of the condominium at The Alexandra, she maintained that her equity in
CG Bytes as Andrew’s common-law partner, and items she brought for its refurnishing
represented her contribution to the acquisition.
58. Donna testified, that as common-law partners, she and Andrew also supported each other
in their professional pursuits. In this regard, she recounted her support of Andrew both
when Andrew lost his employment with Microsoft. She explained that she provided
financial support by maintaining their home, and emotionally in the decision for him to
take some time to recover from the negative emotions due to his dismissal, before getting
13

into new employment. Also, when he did get involved in another business, accepted that
Renderotica had been purchased for its architecture (despite her difficulty with the adult
content), and supported him when its assets were used to incorporate CG Bytes. She
personally involved herself in testing the equipment to see if Andrew could work from TCI.
At this time in 2011, she placed him on her health benefits.
59. Donna stated that it was while she was getting numbers run for her early retirement, in
January 2016, as part of her planning to join Andrew in TCI, that it was revealed that
although they qualified as common-law partners, and the Plaintiff had placed Andrew on
her medical benefits, she had not done so formally. She was required to do so, and for both
she and Andrew to fill out pertinent forms as common-law partners: Pension Benefit
Waiver Form and Homestead Release Form. The parties had not commenced any
discussion on separation, all of the documentation being what was required to regularise
their status for her pension numbers to be run. Thus, it was at this time that Donna placed
Andrew on her pension plan. Andrew filled out the Workmen’s’ Compensation Board
Marriage/Common-law Breakdown Form on 27 January 2016, but did not submit it. on
that form, in the space where he was to supply the last date of cohabitation, he wrote 1 July
2013. The Plaintiff took issue with the Workmen’s’ Compensation Board over the word
‘cohabitation’ rather than ‘separation’ appearing on the form. The form was subsequently
officially changed, and ‘cohabitation’ was replaced with ‘separation’.
60. On Donna’s evidence, what had begun as the process to determine her pension numbers
for retirement was transformed into a process when barely two months later, the parties the
couple descended into a separation. In this process of separation, Andrew waived his rights
to half of the Plaintiff’s pension as a common-law partner. He also signed the Homestead
Release Form by which he released his homestead rights in their home acquired as Donna’s
common-law partner.
61. It is thus the Donna’s case that from 1998, until 1 March 2016, (as recorded in their
Separation Agreement), they cohabited in a conjugal common-law relationship which was
unbroken, even when they lived in different countries: Canada, USA and TCI.
End of Common-law Relationship
62. Donna maintained that the common-law relationship between her and Andrew ended after
February 2016 when she discovered his infidelity, but that they had agreed to use 1 March
14

2016 in the Separation Agreement. Thus, did she reject the 1 July 2013 date asserted by the
Mavis, averring that it was the date Andrew communicated to the tax authorities as his date
of non-residence in Canada, rather than the date they actually separated their relationship.
Explaining this Canada non-residency, Donna averred that it was in a bid to avoid paying
tax in Canada (a circumstance that required the Deceased to be absent from Canada for one
hundred and eighty-three days in the year), that Andrew commenced the process of
securing residence in the TCI. Furthermore, there was the added motivation that property
acquired in TCI, would provide them with a ‘snow-bird’ home, away from the harsh winter
months in Manitoba. Thus, when she agreed in the process of negotiation, to use 1 July
2013 as the end of cohabitation and 1 March as the separation date, it was to appease
Andrew who because of tax implications arising from his declaration on non-residence
from that date to the Canada Revenue Authority (CRA), was unwilling to move forward
with their discussion unless she agreed for them to that date in those formal documents.
However, Andrew signed the Pension Waiver Form which had 1 March 2016 as the
separation date for the computation of her pension and Andrew’s entitlement. He also
signed the Workmen’s Compensation Board Marriage/Common-law Breakdown Form
which had a separation date of 1 March 2016, and he signed their Separation Agreement in
which they both agreed to use 1 March 2016 as the date they decided to live ‘separate and
apart’ from each other.
The Separation Agreement

Expert Evidence
102. Mr. Pinsky KC, and Ms. Sherree Walder, practising lawyers in Manitoba were asked
to provide responses as experts on specific questions on Manitoba law separately, by the
Plaintiff and the Defendant respectively. They provided their separate responses, as well as
a joint statement outlining their areas of agreement and disagreement on the law in

Their answers to relevant questions will be considered in the matters with which this court
is engaged in the discussion which follows.
Issues
103. The matters raised by the pleadings broadly concern the continued validity of
Andrew’s Will; the effect of the provisions of s. 18(4)(e) of the Wills Act of Manitoba on
the devises made to the Plaintiff under the Will; the effect of the Separation Agreement,
and whether the equitable doctrine of probate laches asserted by the Defendant would
applies in this case.
Although at the submissions hearing, I indicated that I would condense the issues helpfully
set out by Mr. Misick KC, it is now my considered view that coherence and comprehensive
dealing with the matter will be better achieved, if the issues are given consideration in the
manner proposed by Misick KC.
104. I therefore adopt the said issues and set them out as follows:
1. Were the Plaintiff and Andrew in a common-law relationship? (The Common-law
Relationship Issue)
2. If they were in a common-law relationship, when did it end? (The Separation Date Issue)
25

3. Did the Separation Agreement revoke the Will? (The Revocation Issue)
4. Should the Separation Agreement be rectified? (The Rectification Issue)
5. Is the defence of probate laches applicable? (The Laches Issue)
6. Is the Defendant entitled to her counterclaim?
Discussion
The Common-law Issue
105. In their pleadings, the parties were joined on the issue of the existence of a common-
law relationship between Andrew and Donna for the Defendant denied the Plaintiff’s
assertion that she had been Andrew’s common-law partner.
106. In this court’s determination of that issue, regard must first be had to the clear and
unequivocal recital in the couple’s Separation Agreement, that they had, until they
commenced to live separate and apart from each other on 1 March 2016, been in a common-
law relationship.
107. In their Separation Agreement, Andrew and Donna stated that they were in a
common law relationship. Mr. Pinsky KC and Ms. Walder who gave evidence on the law
of Manitoba, were agreed in their joint report: that a Manitoba Court would likely accept
what the parties had stated in their Separation agreement as a statement of fact, rather than
go behind the written Agreement, to establish that fact. Indeed, Mr. Pinsky KC was most
clear, that it was only where there was no Separation Agreement setting out the fact of a
common-law relationship, that a Manitoba Court would be likely to apply the factors, set
out in Molodowich v. Penttinen1 to determine its existence, being: shared shelter,
sexual/personal behavior, services, social activities, economic support, children, and
societal perception.
108. Although the Defendant has denied the existence of a common-law relationship
between the parties, she has not challenged the validity of the Separation Agreement, which
recites a common-law relationship between the parties; the Defendant’s challenge has been
1 (1980) CanLII 1537
26

limited to the date of separation which was recorded in the Separation Agreement as 1 July
2016.
109. It would therefore seem that the evidence adduced by both sides would have been
led in vain save for this one thing: the Defendant’s assertion that the account Andrew gave
to her, contradicts what was stated in the Separation Agreement, which Andrew allegedly
entered into, without independent legal advice.
110. The Plaintiff relied on her claim of a common-law partnership with Andrew, and
the end of it in 2016, to counter the Defendant’s attempt to oust her as a beneficiary under
Andrew’s Will on the operation section 18(4)(e) of the Wills Act of Manitoba. As the issue
is joined on the pleadings, and evidence to establish (or deny) its existence has been led
extensively, it is my considered view that this is an occasion to go behind the Separation
Agreement, to examine the evidence adduced in support of, or against the existence of a
common law relationship and for the court to come to its own conclusions which may
simply reinforce what the parties stated in their Separation Agreement, or negate it.
111. I will examine the evidence led in the light of the said Molodowich factors.
The Plaintiff’s evidence on her common-law relationship with Andrew, outlined a conjugal
relationship from 1998 when Andrew moved into Donna’s house, until they decided to end
their relationship in 2016. She spoke of how they ordered their family home, finances, work
life and the career and emotional support she gave to Andrew; how they related to their
children and grandchildren; how they held their properties with an understanding of their
entitlement to each other’s properties under Manitoba law; how it was that Andrew moved
to TCI and the fact that their conjugal relationship continued after that relocation with them
maintaining an attitude of fidelity (until Andrew did not, which led to the end of their
relationship), Andrew spending long months and holidays in their family home while she,
visited with him from time to time in TCI as her employment permitted. She also
demonstrated how each other’s family acknowledged their relationship by including the
other in funeral programmes of Andrew’s father and Donna’s stepfather.
112. The Plaintiff’s evidence was corroborated in material detail by Chris and Tara, the
children of the household at various times. They gave credible evidence regarding the
family life they had with Andrew, beginning from when Andrew moved into their home,
in 1998; Andrew and Donna’s unconventional exchange of vows, and their conjugal
27

relationship sharing the same bedroom; their work around the house, Donna sharing in
Andrew’s work at his basement office, the parenting role Andrew assumed in relation to
both Chris and Tara which included in the early years, school visits, homework assistance;
financial and emotional support of Tara as a young adult living on her own pursuing studies,
and his nurturing in the role of grandfather to Cobain and Pepper how they related as a
family including sharing meals together; playing games together as a family. In early 2016,
the evidence that Andrew continued to relate to Tara as a father is evident from their
conversation. In this conversation with Tara, he communicated his plan to take Cobain and
Pepper to Disney word when they were eight and five years. In that interesting
conversation. Tara reminded him of her trip with Andrew, Donna and Chris to that same
place when she was eleven years old. Andrew promised that the whole family would go
there in March 2016. In his humorous way he stated: “Studios in Orlando…"We will rent
a big ass condo and bring everyone down for a week."
113. The evidence of Kathryn Payne, Andrew and Donna’s neighbour for many years,
related to how Andrew and Donna presented themselves as a couple in a conjugal
relationship, and as a family with their children. As a family friend who went in and out of
their house, as they did hers, her evidence of the sleeping arrangements of Andrew and the
Plaintiff: that they shared a bedroom upstairs, and that Andrew’s office was in the basement
where she had been a number of times, carried weight in the support it gave to the Plaintiff’s
case. While she conceded during cross-examination from examining a photograph, that
Andrew had not, as she had stated, always worn a gold band to symbolise his union with
the Plaintiff, she came out as a witness of truth whose testimony regarding the conjugal
relationship of Andrew and the Plaintiff, and also her testimony that Andrew used the
basement as his office and not his place of residence, should carry weight with this court
regarding how they presented themselves as a couple to society and friends.
114. So should the evidence of the Financial Advisor mostly documentary, which was
concerned with their financial affairs. Her evidence that Andrew and Donna did joint
financial planning was supported by documentary evidence of contemporaneous writing
about the linking of their accounts with the Financial Advisor, although their accounts were
held as individuals and not a joint account, so that she sometimes met with them as a
couple, and at other times, individually. She also testified that the acquisition, fixing and
28

flipping of the Hetherington property which was in Andrew’s sole name, was a business
venture and not to provide different accommodation for Andrew. Indeed, her answer in
cross-examination, was that Andrew made the Hetherington property his principal
residence to avoid paying tax on it. She called it tax evasion. Lastly, she stated that the
purchase of real estate in TCI, was part of the couple’s retirement planning.
115. Documentary evidence that supported the Plaintiff’s claim of a common-law
relationship with Andrew, included Andrew’s entry in his application to become a client of
the Financial Advisor, that Donna was his common-law spouse. There were also other
documentary evidence of Andrew declaring that Donna was his common-law partner in the
Workmen’s Compensation Board Marriage/Common-law Breakdown Form, the Pension
Release Form, and the Homestead Release Form in which he indicated that he had gained
homestead rights in 725 Pritchard Farm Road, as Donna’s common-law partner.
In a formal application on the Manulife Securities for tax-free savings account application,
however, although Andrew declared Donna to be his beneficiary, he checked a box that
stated that he was neither married nor living common-law, and furthermore, indicated that
Donna was his friend.
116. What would have been revelatory contrary evidence fails to assume that
significance, in the light of this instructive circumstance: until January 2016 when Donna
commenced her pre-retirement project of having her numbers ran, she did not know or
view herself as a common-law partner. In fact, she said so in an email to her colleague
Cindy Graham with whom she had a discussion on her relationship status with Andrew in
relation to her retirement. The Plaintiff’s curious exchange with Cindy Graham, was in
spite of her eighteen-year relationship with Andrew, that qualified as a common-law
partnership, unregistered though it was. Andrew and Donna had conducted themselves
accordingly in the way they lived, and they undoubtedly qualified to be common-law
partners - as qualification for that status in Manitoba required only three years’
cohabitation. It is to be noted that it was after this that Donna informed Andrew that they
were considered common-law partners. It is therefore not surprising that Andrew who had
been divorced from his wife Richelle, and living with the Plaintiff for some nine years
before he filled out that form and who in 2016 was informed that such was his relationship
status with Donna, would indicate that Donna was his friend, rather than common-law
29

partner, as they had it seems, not assigned a formal description to their relationship. That
singular circumstance could therefore, not override the overwhelming evidence of the
existence of a common-law relationship.
117. In my view, Andrew and Donna’s conjugal relationship, their parenting, as well as
grandparenting, their presentation to family and friends as a couple, and a family with their
children (Chris and Tara), their family life, evidence of their joint financial planning
including retirement planning, Donna’s support of Andrew with her benefits in the times
he was out of formal employment, was evidence that satisfied the Molodowich factors.
This is quite apart from the fact that in 2016, while filling out forms for release of pension,
and homestead rights, Andrew indicated that he was the common-law partner of Donna.
118. By contrast the evidence presented by the Defendant who denied the existence of a
common-law relationship between Andrew and the Plaintiff lacked credibility. It seemed
to me, form answers the Defendant gave during cross-examination, that she did not know
much about Andrew’s life with Donna, and that the account she gave of confidences he
shared with her, was contrived. The quality of the Defendant’s evidence was much assailed
by cross-examination. Indeed, the Defendant’s entire case of there not having been a
common-law relationship at all, between Andrew and the Plaintiff, was inconsistent with
the case she had put forward in the earlier proceedings at the Manitoba Court. Confronted
with the discrepancy, the Defendant had no explanation for it. It therefore appeared that her
pleaded denial of a common-law relationship was an afterthought, introduced in the present
proceedings for an undisclosed reason.
119. I reproduce the cross-examination of this point:
“Q. And you swore three affidavits in the Manitoba proceedings, did you not?
A. Yes.
Q. And in none of those affidavits did you deny or did you assert that Donna and Andrew
were never in a common-law relationship, is that not correct?
A. Yes, I guess so.”
117. In other cross-examination:
“Q. Now, having regard to your answers to all of those questions, do you still maintain that Donna
and Andrew were never in a common-law relationship?
30

A. It would be undecided between 2005 and 2010, whether there was a true common-law

be a common-law relationship when he said he was living in the basement. It didn’t seem to me
that was a good common-law relationship at all.
Q. So that is your assessment of it, that is your take on it?
A. Yeah, and he didn’t own anything there. He was just living there.”
“Q. And since seeing the separation agreement between Andrew and Donna, which was signed in

118. Her witnesses fared no better.
119. What stood out regarding Robert’s testimony, was his departure from the Defendant’s case
which denied the existence of a common-law relationship. Robert admitted that Andrew had
moved into the Plaintiff’s house in 1998 and that they had had a conjugal relationship. He admitted
that what they had was a common-law relationship, except that in his reckoning, it commenced in
2005, and ended in 2011 (although he also stood by a 2013 end of cohabitation between them).
120. Rebecca Strohl understandably could not give any evidence on the subject, beyond
asserting that Andrew had told her of his relationship with the Plaintiff which he
allegedly said had broken down, and had been his motivation for moving to TCI.
121. Regarding the quality of her evidence on the things she confidently asserted, she was
clearly taken aback when she was confronted during cross-examination with text
messages from Andrew to Donna, which showed an existing relationship between them
at the time she believed their relationship was long ended. There was also the
31

discrepancy between what Andrew had told her regarding the Plaintiff’s visit in
February 2016 (that she came to seek for reconciliation and was turned away) and what
he wrote to the Plaintiff, apologising for ruining her surprise visit. Her own knowledge
gap was revealed by her evident consternation over these revelations, and thus failed to
provide credible support for the Defendant’s case on the matter of Andrew’s alleged
cessation of cohabitation with the Plaintiff following his move to TCI in 2013.
122.The quality of Terri Lindsay’s evidence was severely affected by the internal
inconsistencies in it which affected her credibility as a witness of truth. Much of Terri
Lindasy’s evidence, was contradicted by credible documentary evidence such as emails
from Andrew to third parties in TCI in 2012, 2013 all through to 2015 regarding his
relationship with the Plaintiff (whom he described as his wife), their family relationship
with Bozley, and their life even after the move to TCI. Her evidence is also contradicted
by the Skype conversation between Andrew and Tara which did not paint Terri in a
positive light, and in which Andrew stated that he had not spoken to Terri (voice call) in
almost five years. It was clear from Andrew’s own communication: “Oh boy, About to
start a call with Terri…First time in five years…Yes, I somehow managed to avoid a
voice call with her or that long.” that she had no favour in his eyes, and it was not likely
that he would confide his personal troubles to her. Terri Lindsay’s account of her
relationship with Andrew, and the information he allegedly gave her of his living
arrangements, thus lacked credibility, and failed to support the Defendant’s case that
there had been no common-law relationship between Andrew and the Plaintiff, that he
and Donna had had a bad relationship, or that when he moved to TCI in 2013, he
intended it to be the end of his relationship with Donna.
123. It is difficult to reconcile Andrew’s documented movements in TCI and Manitoba in
2013 (per emails), with Ms. Stingl’s narrative of an Andrew presenting as single, already
settled in TCI, and dating other women in 2013 when they allegedly met. It was obvious
that Ms. Stingl was either deliberately telling an untruth, or that she got the years mixed
up, for she conflated Andrew’s time of residence at The Atrium in 2013, with his time
at The Alexandra which commenced when the condominium was purchased in April
2015. This anachronism dented the reliability of her evidence regarding her alleged close
32

friendship which involved sharing his confidence, and affected her credibility as a
witness. It thus failed to support the case put forward by the Defendant in this regard.
124. In evaluating the evidence of the Plaintiff regarding the existence of a common-law
relationship with Andrew, I have while, while considering the evidence of the Plaintiff,
also had regard to evidence that was adduced to demonstrate that there was not. These
are: the apparent lack of holding joint finances (the matter of the removal of the
Plaintiff’s name from their joint account in 2011); lack of joint property ownership (the
acquisition of properties by Andrew in his sole name); the indication on Andrew’s tax
return in 2009 that he paid the sum of $9600 for the year as rent to Donna, and the
Plaintiff’s own communication with Andrew that his ‘rent’ was reduced to $700 (their
living arrangements).
Joint Finances and Financial Support
125. In contemporaneous notes made by the Financial Advisor in 2009, after Andrew left
Microsoft, the Financial Advisor’s record of her meeting with Andrew included the note:
“"Group benefits will use Donna's plan” The evidence also shows that when Andrew
left Hewlett Packard in 2011, the Plaintiff placed Andrew on her health benefits. It
seems to me therefore that the lack of a joint bank account from 2011, should not
without more, lead to a conclusion of the non-existence of, or a breakdown of their
common-law partnership for, even as the Plaintiff’s name was removed from their joint
bank account, she placed Andrew on her health benefits in that same year (2011).
Andrew used the health benefits through to as late as April 2016. In my judgment, the
Plaintiff’s explanation for removing her name from the Cambrian Credit Union account,
being, that the purchase of Renderotica as a business, Andrew having left Hewlett
Packard, occasioned some trepidation in her regarding their assets - was entirely
believable. The Financial Advisor could not attest to why Donna separated her banking
but in that context, during cross-examination, she indicated that Andrew did not want to
have their accounts joined in view of the development loans he was accessing at TD
Bank. This was at the time for property development as a business venture.
126.The Plaintiff stated that a further protection of their assets was Andrew’s use of the
Hetherington property as his address for the business, instead of their home address. In
33

the absence of contrary evidence, I would accept that the said matters would not without
more, impugn the Plaintiff’s case that they made financial decisions together.
Property Ownership
127. That the lack of property ownership should not be a gauge for a determination as to
whether they had a common-law relationship, is evidenced by the fact that throughout
their life together, in a common-law relationship (admitted by Robert), the couple never
owned property jointly and conducted their financial/property affairs separately. This
what happened when Andrew went to live in Virginia when the couple carried on (as
Robert admits), a conjugal relationship, Andrew acquired his apartment and vehicles in
his own name. The properties he later developed as a business (2006 and 2010), were
also in his sole name and with his own finances, although on the evidence of the
Financial Advisor, the Plaintiff was involved physically in the property supervision part,
not doing the heavy lifting but being present at the site as much as Andrew, to her
knowledge. Andrew in his emails indicated in 2012 to Peter Crawford, that he was
looking for real estate in TCI together with the Plaintiff. In 2013, he wrote to Mr. Wattiez
regarding the rental of the apartment at The Atrium in that same vein, and in 2015, he
wrote to Design Studio indicating that he and the Plaintiff were making decorating
decisions for their condominium at The Alexandra, yet all the real estate was acquired
in Andrew’s name.
It is also instructive that 725 Pritchard Farm Road where they lived and Andrew returned
to from TCI through to early 2016, remained in Donna’s sole name, even though Andrew
recognised that he had a share in it as a common-law partner, and demonstrated that
belief by releasing his acquired rights as a common-law partner, in the couple’s
separation. These many examples of individual ownership and conduct of affairs,
reinforce my view that is how they ordered their lives as a couple, rather than an
intention to exclude the other partner.
Living Arrangements (Tenancy)
128. The assertion that Andrew’s status in the Plaintiff’s home was as a tenant in the
basement of 725 Pritchard Farm Road, was anchored on two pieces of evidence:
Andrew’s tax return of 2009 in which he stated that that he paid rent to the Plaintiff, and
34

the Plaintiff’s email to Andrew of 12 October 2008 in which she told him that his ‘rent’
had been reduced to $700. In my judgment, neither provide conclusive evidence of the
matter alleged. I say so for the following reasons:
129. It is not clear why Andrew indicated that he paid rent to the Plaintiff in his 2009 tax
return, however, it seems to me that as that entry was intended to secure a credit for him,
it should not without more, be accepted as evidence of a landlord/tenant relationship
between Andrew and the Plaintiff, especially as there was no evidence adduced of a
corresponding entry of rent receipt as income in the Plaintiff’s own tax return.
130. The Plaintiff’s explanation for her use of the word ‘rent’ in quotes in her email to
Andrew: that it referred to his household contribution, was believable. This is because
of the context of that scathing email in which Donna expressed dissatisfaction over how
they were ordering their household finances as a couple. The email had a sneering tone,
and appeared to be aimed at pointing out the irony of Andrew complaining about Chris’
failure to contribute to the household budget when he himself was apparently not doing
any better.
131. The email read:
Here is a copy for your records of my budget. This may explain to you why I am on my
toes and concerned (not complaining as you are quick to point out) about money, savings,
retirement funds and keeping a roof over everyone’s head…
There is no need to sit down and review as I have asked you for so many years…
As a result and to make it fair that you keep your contributions to the barest minimum
this email advises you that your ‘rent’ will be decreased to $700…have fun with your
extra $1200 per year. This can buy someone else a great deal of flowers …and not from
Sobeys:).
From this point on can you please verbally tell me the day upon which you will move
money to my account.
I know that the majority of homes with a couple residing in it do not finance their life
this way…As for your comments about Chris, I agree to a point and have been an enabler
but it seems to me to be so hypacritical [sic] coming from you as I have enabled you.
Enjoy your Thanks Giving Turkey today…as it is on me”[emphasis supplied]
35

132. In my judgment, rather than providing evidence of a tenancy, as of a negotiation
between a landlord and her tenant, the email provided evidence of domestic
arrangements gone awry, and the Plaintiff’s dissatisfaction with Andrew’s attitude
toward this own contributions to the running of the home. In my view, any other
interpretation of the email would be artificial, given the context and the high
improbability of a landlord discussing her own finances with a mere tenant, as the
Defendant asserted.
133. Nor was the Defendant’s case advanced by the excerpt of the Plaintiff’s email of 6
October 2014 of which the Defendant made capital - Donna to Andrew:
"Good morning. A couple of quick questions, and sorry to bother. I wanted to run a DVD
in the lower level your [apartment]t" --”.
In my view, while this piece of evidence taken in isolation may suggest that Andrew had
an apartment in the basement, it seems to me that other evidence would belie this. This
other evidence consists of the evidence of Chris, Tara and Kathryn Payne the neighbour,
regarding Andrew’s use of the basement as an office and not living quarters, as well as the
evidence of the Financial Advisor whose husband renovated the basement into an office
for Andrew. Indeed, it was corroborated by the Defendant, who apparently off script,
admitted that when she went down to the basement with Andrew it was an office. This was
interesting given the narrative that the Defendant held onto, but which was fraught with
inconsistencies. It is worth pointing out inconsistencies regarding Andrew’s alleegd
occupation of the basement as his living quarters and when that happened. While both
Mavis and Robert asserted that Andrew went to live in the basement in 2005 when he
returned from Virginia Robert later stated that he did so in 2008, having stopped sharing
Donna’s bed. Then he stated that it was in 2011, lastly, that Andrew went to live in the
basement in 2013, and after relocation to TCI, returned from time to time to live in the
basement where he paid rent as a tenant. The Defendant also changed the time Andrew
allegedly went to live in the basement form 2005, to 2008 when Andrew’s father died, and
then to 2013.
134. In my view, the conduct of Andrew after this email, which was that in April 2015, as
he had done in 2012-2013 with the process of renting the apartment at the Atrium,
Andrew continued to hold himself out with the Plaintiff to third parties (Design Studio)
36

in TCI, as a couple in the process of refurnishing family-acquired property at The
Alexandra.
135. Having considered all the evidence: the Plaintiff’s evidence of their living
arrangements, their family life with the children of the household (Chris and Tara), and
later with grandchildren (Cobain and Pepper); their financial decisions and support, their
social presentation to their extended families (recorded in funeral brochures of Andrew’s
father and the Plaintiff’s stepfather), their interactions with their neighbour Kathryn
Payne and their Financial Advisor, as well as Andrew’s nurturing relationship with the
children and grandchildren (Chris, Tara, Cobain and Pepper), were all consistent with
the incidents of a common-law relationship as set out in Molodowich. This is reinforced
by the weight of documentary evidence in which Andrew acknowledged his common-
law relationship with the Plaintiff. These were: Andrew’s emails in which he described
the Plaintiff as his wife with whom he made decisions on the
rental/acquisition/refurnishing of real property in TCI, formal documents being his
application form to become a client with the Manulife dealer/broker, the Homestead
Release Form, the WCB Marriage/Common-law Relationship Breakdown Form and the
Separation Agreement.
136. It seems to me that on the balance, the Plaintiff’s evidence of a common-law
relationship with Andrew was established by overwhelming evidence from
corroborating witnesses, and documentary evidence, confirming, rather than negating
what she and Andrew as parries to the Separation Agreement, set out as the status they
were ending by their Agreement.
The Separation Date Issue
137. Mr. Pinsky KC and Ms. Walder, the experts gave evidence on the law in Manitoba as
applied in the Courts of Manitoba, stated that the separation date is the valuation date
for asset equalization in Manitoba. The experts agreed in their Joint Statement, that:
“Absent specific agreement in a valid separation agreement, or Court order, whether
parties are living separate and apart must be determined based on the factors set out in the
37

case law including the test in the Molodowich case, and analysis of whether the criteria
for cessation of a common-law relationship have been” [my emphasis]
They maintained this position throughout their extensive cross-examination. Mr. Pinsky
KC was clear as he was cross-examined on his report, that the separation date was often
subject to negotiation, but that once the parties had come to an agreement on the effective
date of their separation, a Manitoba Court would not resort to the Molodowich factors but
would accept, and give effect to the date the parties had chosen as their date of separation.
138. In their 31 August 2016 Separation Agreement, the Plaintiff and Andrew set out 1
March 2016 as the date they lived separate and apart from each other. As observed
earlier, the validity of the Separation Agreement is not under challenge by either party.
The separation date of 1 March 2016 recorded in the Separation Agreement, should
then, in accordance with the law and practice in the courts of Manitoba, have sufficed
for a finding that the date the parties thereto set down as when they began to live separate
and apart from each other, was 1 March 2016.
139. However, the Defendant seeks rectification of the Agreement, for the separation date to
be altered to a date significantly earlier than 1 March 2016.
140. A claim for rectification is one of two circumstances that according to Mr. Pinsky KC,
will engage a Manitoba Court, to go behind a separation date in a Separation Agreement.
I will therefore, having regard to the Defendant’s claim, go ahead to consider the
evidence adduced for and against the 1 March 2016 separation date as the effective date
of the separation of the common-law relationship of Andrew and the Plaintiff.
141. Although the Defendant, in the earlier proceedings in the Manitoba Court testified that
she did not know why the parties chose the 1 March 2016 date, stated in these
proceedings that it was: “…because of the Workmen's Compensation paperwork.”.
There is no evidence in support of this position that appears to be mere surmise.
142. There is no doubt that the separation date has assumed a pivotal dimension in this case,
as an earlier date would mean that Andrew died more than three years after the parties
separated, and the significance of this is: by operation of s. 18(4)(e) of the Wills Act of
Manitoba, the devises made to the Plaintiff (the sole beneficiary of Andrew’s Will),
would be revoked, the law deeming that the Plaintiff had predeceased Andrew.
38

143. It is the Defendant’s case in this regard, that any common-law relationship between
Andrew and the Plaintiff, ended on 1 July 2013 when Andrew relocated to TCI where
he became resident, separating himself permanently from the Plaintiff.
144. The Defendant’s narrative was that Andrew had not been happy in his relationship with
the Plaintiff and had devised ways to escape from it. Thus, it was her evidence that
having failed to leave the Plaintiff by obtaining employment in the USA, and upon
returning to Canada, having failed to move into his own acquired houses (River’s Drive
and Hetherington Avenue, which he sold), he seized the opportunity offered by a
relocation to TCI to finally rid himself o Donna, who was unwilling to relocate.
145. She alleged that before moving to TCI in 2013, Andrew communicated to his whole
family, (including his two grandmothers), that he intended the move to be permanent,
and that Donna who did not wish to relocate to TCI, would continue to live and work in
Manitoba. The move was therefore a permanent separation from Donna.
146. The Defendant also relied on Andrew’s emails by which he and the Plaintiff discussed
and negotiated their separation date. In his email communication, Andrew had insisted
that his cohabitation with Donna ended on 1 July 2013 when his principal place of
residence changed to TCI. The Plaintiff also, in her email of 4 July 2016 to Andrew,
stated: “We agree for your convenience that end cohab is July 2013 and separation date
is March 2016”.
147. The Defendant’s case was supported by Robert, who maintained that the Separation
Agreement date of 1 March 2016 was incorrect. It was his surmise that the 1 March
2016 date on the Separation Agreement was artificially engineered by the Plaintiff to get
out of potential fraud risks for placing Andrew on her health benefits in 2011, without
declaring him her common-law partner, and additionally, filing tax returns as a divorced
person rather than as a common-law spouse.
148. Robert alleged that Andrew had communicated to him, his intention of permanently
separating from the Plaintiff with his 2013 move to TCI, and that Andrew’s conduct
thereafter which was: seeking residence, dating other women (as he alleged), confirmed
that intention. He explained that although they had commenced to live ‘separate and
apart’ from each other from 2013, cohabitation having ceased with his move to TCI,
Andrew returned to Canada again and again, to fulfil an immigration requirement for
39

him to be in Canada for periods of time while he was working on the residence process,
and that he lived at 725 Pritchard Farm Road because of his friendship with Donna, and
not pursuant to any common-law relationship. He alleged that Andrew occasionally
stayed in hotels in Manitoba.
149.No evidence of an immigration requirement for Andrew to return to Canada was
provided. Robert’s evidence also that Andrew occasionally stayed in hotels in Winnipeg,
was supported with an email from Andrew to a lawyer in TCI who was handling his real
estate purchase in 2015. That obviously self-serving email in which Andrew stated that
he had incurred hotel expenses due to the late closing of his real estate purchase after he
had received a promise of reimbursement of expenses, was hardly evidence that would
contradict the overwhelming evidence that Andrew stayed in 725 Pritchard Farm Road
for the summer months, holidays, and for family events.
150. These pieces of evidence - offered to explain Andrew’s post-2013 conduct to fit the
narrative of a cessation of cohabitation with the Plaintiff after 2013 - were neither
credible nor consistent with the content of Andrew’s emails, and text messages, to the
Plaintiff and emails to third parties in TCI, as well as the pictures of the family life he
continued to have with the Plaintiff and her children and grandchildren through to
Christmas 2015.
151. Robert Clark’s evidence did not stand the scrutiny of cross-examination. It became clear
that as he had previously told the Manitoba Court, he had not had a close relationship
with Andrew during the period of his relationship with the Plaintiff, despite his attempt
to establish the contrary in these proceedings. He also admitted during cross-
examination in the Manitoba Court that in the period after Andrew’s relationship with
Donna ended, by his reckoning, their conversations were limited to three or four times
in the year. Thus, contrary to his assertions that he had been given first-hand information
by Andrew of the things of which he spoke, it was clear that it was not so. It is small
wonder that his evidence was demonstrably fraught with internal inconsistencies.
Examples are his evidence that Andrew lived in the basement from 2008 as a tenant,
staying at that address only because he did not wish to upset the children; that Andrew
told him that he had ceased to share a bed with the Plaintiff, and related to the Plaintiff
as a friend. These pieces of evidence were inconsistent with his insistence that the
40

common-law relationship ended in 2011, or the end of cohabitation in 2013, brought
about by the Andrew’s move to TCI, another narrative, to which he clung.
152. Robert acknowledged that he relied on the documentary evidence retrieved from the
laptop as one of the sources of his testimony before this court. The retrievals
undoubtedly provided facts, but the narrative he wove from the said facts was his, and
the attempt to ascribe to that narrative, the authenticity of Andrew’s communication, is
what tainted his evidence with falsehood. An example of this is this: from bank records
that showed that in 2011, the Plaintiff’s name was removed from the bank account she
had held jointly with Andrew, and especially from Andrew’s email to the Plaintiff during
their early communication about their separation date to fill out the form from the
Workmen’s Compensation Board in which he insisted on 1 July 2013 as the end of their
cohabitation (as opposed to the end of their relationship), Robert drew the conclusion
that Andrew moved to TCI to disengage from Donna. He did so without any background
information, such as has been provided by the Plaintiff and corroborated by the Financial
Advisor. Robert’s interpretation of documents without context, formed the basis of the
Defendant’s case which he supported with the same narrative he had created, averring
that he had received the information first-hand information from Andrew, although in
previous proceedings in the Manitoba Court, he had described his relationship with
Andrew, during his eighteen-year relationship with Donna, as distant.
153. That Robert acknowledged in the Manitoba proceedings that Andrew had told him in
2012 that he was moving permanently to TCI, and that it had to do with avoidance of
tax liabilities in Canada, is something he kept from this court where he doggedly asserted
that it was to get away from Donna that Andrew moved to TCI. This was an allegation
that supported the narrative Robert created from Andrew’s insistence in an email, that 1
July 2013 was the end of their cohabitation, a date the Plaintiff on her own showing,
agreed to, because it was easier to appease than to stand her ground.
154. That narrative was also reinforced by the obfuscation of the matter of a separation date
communicated by Andrew in his Retainer Agreement with Terri Mestdagh. While it was
clear, from that document, that Andrew’s understanding of separation was physical, and
not relational, the Defendant’s narrative as supported by Robert insisted that it was a
communication of his breakup with Donna in 2013. The clear misrepresentation of this
41

matter was evident from the fact that in writing down the periods of separation, Andrew
also referred to the period of his absence from Manitoba while he was in Virginia in the
same vein, although Robert acknowledged that the common-law relationship continued
while Andrew was in Virginia (even as he also asserted that the common-law
relationship began in 2005 after his return from Virginia).
155. On the other hand, the evidence of the Plaintiff, that Andrew’s relocation to TCI was
tax-motivated, to achieve the one-hundred-and-eighty-three-day absence from Canada
which would qualify him as a non-resident person for tax purposes and provide a route
to avoid the payment of Canada tax - with the added motivation of acquiring a snowbird
home for him and for the Plaintiff, was corroborated in material detail by the credible
evidence of their Financial Advisor. The documentary evidence of the Financial
Advisor’s meeting notes, produced contemporaneously with her meetings with Andrew,
as well as documentary evidence from KPMG, provided further corroboration of the
tax motivation for Andrew’s move to TCI. Interestingly as I have observed, Robert, also
corroborated it, strengthening the Plaintiff’s account. I reproduce an excerpt from

“Q. So you had indicated in your February affidavit that you had discussions with your

156.That there was an added motivation of acquiring a second home was supported by

regarding the rental of The Atrium for him and the Plaintiff; to Olga in August 2013

relationship with the Plaintiff whom he described as his wife, and a family relationship
42

with Tara who was to meet up with them in TCI and whom he described to Olga as their
twenty-four year old daughter.
157. The evidence is ample, that in 2015, which is, two years after Andrew’s 1 July 2013
‘relocation’ date, the Plaintiff and Andrew continued to carry on a conjugal relationship
although they lived in two different countries. There is therefore no credible evidence
that Andrew intended to move to TCI to separate himself from Donna in 2013.
158. That Andrew and Donna’s continued conjugal relationship long after the 2013 TCI
