160.It seems to me that the evidence overwhelmingly tilts the balance towards a finding that
on 1 July 2013 when Andrew ‘relocated’ to TCI, he did not end his conjugal relationship
with the Plaintiff, and that as they did when Andrew relocated to Washington DC, USA,
they continued with their conjugal relationship, even though Andrew had declared
himself non-resident in Canada.
161. In any event (this is uncontroverted), although Andrew had signed a lease at The Atrium
which commenced on 1 July 2013 (the date he conveniently used as evidence of the end
of his cohabitation), Andrew did not in fact, leave Manitoba for TCI until 13 July 2013,
and even so, that he stayed for two weeks and returned to Manitoba to live in the family
house at 725 Pritchard Farm Road. Andrew in fact, did not apply for residency in TCI
until September 2013, and it is not clear when he received his residence permit. In
August 2013, although Andrew and the Plaintiff intended to return to TCI, together, only
Andrew returned to receive Tara who stayed with him for one month.
162. The evidence regarding these matters was compelling: that after he relocated to TCI,
Andrew spent a lot of time in Manitoba in the 725 Pritchard Farm Road house (five to
six months in the year, as well as holidays) This was consistent with what he had told
45

Mr. Peter Crawford Smith, the TCI realtor: that he and Donna intended to spend six
months in TCI and the warmer months in Canada.
163.The evidence also showed that the Plaintiff visited with him in TCI. Andrew also
consistently - from 2013 to 2015 - held out the Plaintiff to third parties in TCI (real
estate people and property furnishers) as his wife, and participant in real estate decisions.
Andrew also gave the impression to third parties in TCI that the purchase of the
condominium at The Alexandra was for him and the Plaintiff and the Plaintiff
participated in homeowner’s meetings at the Alexandra in 2016.
164. Thus, on the evidence, Andrew went back and forth between his Manitoba home and
his rental apartment at TCI. Although he did not spend Christmas 2013 in TCI, he did
so in 2014, and 2015. Family pictures have been exhibited in these proceedings of their
Christmas family celebration which included the dog Bozley). Andrew left for TCI on 6
January 2016, having joined in the early birthday celebration for Cobain on 4 January
2016.
165. Thus, despite his insistence in emails that he had not lived in Canada from 1 July 2013,
which he even communicated to his lawyer Terri Mestdagh who repeated it to Alex
Cudney (Donna’s lawyer) in her email to him of 27 June 2016, and his assertion that he
had ceased cohabiting with the Plaintiff from 1 July 2013 date - which assertion is relied
on by the Defendant in furtherance of her case, it is manifest that Andrew’s own conduct
contradicted that narrative.
166. Indeed, the totality of the evidence contradicts the Defendant’s case, that the couple’s
common-law relationship ended in 2013, while it supports the Plaintiff’s case, that even
though Andrew had declared himself non-resident in Canada, and the Plaintiff continued
to live and work in Canada, the quality of their relationship remained unchanged.
Cohabitation
167. The issue of cohabitation has undergirded the case of the Defendant in this regard, as
the Plaintiff agreed to Andrew’s demand to use the 1 July 2013 date as the end of their
cohabitation, although she differentiated that from the end of their relationship. .
Admittedly, the Plaintiff in that email, qualified her agreement with the words: “for your
convenience”. Her explanation is that she agreed to Andrew’s demand, to appease him
46

and to get him to move the process along. If the Plaintiff’s agreement for ‘convenience’
was all the court had to go by, it would have given some credence to the Defendant’s
case in this regard, as it was a communicated meeting of minds on the subject.
168. It is for this reason that in examining the evidence extrinsic to the Separation Agreement
to determine the separation date, the subject of cohabitation, should receive due
consideration. In Ms. Walder’s report, she discussed the concept of cohabitation, citing
the Canadian case of Kruk v Kruk2, that:
“The law is clear that living under separate households is not a bar to a finding of
‘cohabitation’ for the purposes of a common-law relationship; however, this is a fact
specific exercise. In Kruk, despite there being separate residences, the parties operated as
a shared domestic unit carrying out household chores and domestic projects together”
169. Mr. Pinsky KC responding to a question during cross examination on what constituted
the end of cohabitation, harped on a conjugal relationship as inherent to cohabitation: I
reproduce the exchange:
“Q. Yes. So if the parties were- in in the recitals the parties were referring to the end date
of their relationship, not the end date of cohabitation, which is a different date, then this
document does not establish the end date of cohabitation, does it?
A. So if by that you mean cohabitation like they weren’t in a conjugal relationship, but
were living like roommates at some point after that date, that could be the case.”
170. I reiterate that the evidence demonstrates the existence of a conjugal relationship
between Andrew and the Plaintiff after his move to TCI in 2013. That they also carried
on a family relationship is clear from Andrew hosting Tara - having described her to
Olga the manager of the rental at the Atrium as his daughter; he was present with the
family: the Plaintiff, Chris, Tara, Cobain and Pepper, for Christmas in 2014, and 2015.
Further, an exchange of emails shows that Andrew continued to carry out chores around
the house in Manitoba: in an exchange of emails of the Plaintiff complained of work
around the house in Manitoba that Andrew had not done. By reply, he promptly
promised to return to Manitoba to do it. In January 2016, Andrew was present for an
early celebration of Cobain’s birthday, and on April 10, he was present at Pepper’s
2 [ 2025 MBKB 17]
47

birthday where he and the Plaintiff broke the news of their separation to Chris. Andrew,
like the Plaintiff, called up Tara to speak with her about the separation.
171. It is therefore evident that Andrew and Donna continued to cohabit in a conjugal
relationship, and operated as a family unit with children, Chris and Tara, and with
grandchildren Cobain and Pepper (as an addition in later years), even though they lived
in different countries, so to speak. I qualify that assertion because of Andrew’s presence
in the Manitoba home for long stretches of time: five to six months in the year, as well
as for holidays and family events. This continued until the events of February 2016
which commenced their descent into a separation.
172. Thus, on the evidence, the parties did not cease to cohabit after 1 July 2013, for far from
supporting that assertion, their conduct spoke otherwise. This appears to be so, even if
Andrew for his own purposes (which the Plaintiff described as his ‘convenience’), chose
to harp on that date and demand its use in formal documents. It is to be noted that in her
email of 5 May 2016, as the Plaintiff and Andrew negotiated their separation the Plaintiff
stated: “I know you fret on the separation date however it has nothing to do with when
the condo was purchased or your date of residency. Basically, it is the start date of our
relationship and the end date of which you ended us…”
She also in her email of 4 July 2016 under the head; ‘Confirm Separation Date, Please’,
stated: “Alex just spoke with Terri and now she says you want July of 2013 as a
separation date. Before it was July 2015…we Separated March 2016…2013 was when
you got your residency not when we separated.” Even so, about two hours later of the
same date, Donna agreed “for [Andrew’s] convenience’, to use 1 July 2013 as the end
of cohabitation and 1 March 2016 as the end of their relationship. Andrew also, in his
communication with Terri Mestdagh, stated that he had ended his relationship with
Donna in January 2016. His communication of an end date of cohabitation on 1 July
2013 was an obvious misstatement of the facts, when it apparently suited his purposes
to say so in formal documents while saying differently as he did to Terri Mestdagh where
he gave an end date of his relationship to be January 2016.
173.That Andrew’s tax obligations which had informed his decision to relocate to TCI was
his driving force in any discussion on his residence, is manifest from in his emails to
third parties. In April 2015 he wrote to Karissa Wood complaining that his mail was
48

being delivered to what he called ‘his previous address’. He requested that his mail be
sent to 1800-201 Portage Avenue, his lawyer’s office address. In a later email, clearly
agitated, he wrote: “ …It’s going to be tough for my accountant to prove non-residency
status when you’re still using a Canadian residence I no longer live at”. Yet on the
evidence, he spent only one hundred and twenty-eight days in TCI in 2014, the rest of
the time, he spent at this address including Christmas. In 2015, he spent the summer
months as well as Christmas 2015, through to 5 January 2016, and left the next day.
174.It is my view that the parties never ceased cohabitation, and that Andrew insistence on
the end of cohabitation was motivated by the sole drive to establish himself as a non-
resident of Canada for tax purposes, although in reality he lived in both places and
carried on a conjugal relationship with Donna and a family relationship with Donna and
the children ad grandchildren.
175. But this foray into the hair-splitting adventure of determining cohabitation as against
relationship in my view fails to assist the Defendant’s case which relies on it for the
determination of the date of separation. This is because, whereas cohabitation for three
years is required to establish a common-law unregistered relationship, the provisions of
section 18(4)(e)of the Wills Act which is the basis for the Defendant’s quest for a date
earlier than 1 March 2016 in the Separation Agreement, speaks of ‘separate and apart’,
and not ‘end of cohabitation’.
176. According to Ms. Walder, there is no statutory definition of ‘separate and apart’ in
Manitoba, so that a determination of the fact must be on a case-specific basis. However,
Mr. Pinsky KC, speaking of the important date to determine separation, stated: “The key
date for Manitoba law, the valuation date is the date of separation”. It was his opinion
that negotiated separation dates are not an uncommon feature in Separation Agreements,
and in his view, a Manitoba Court was not likely to go behind what the parties
determined to be their separation date.
177.The evidence establishes that the parties agreed on 1 March 2016 as the date they
commenced to live separate and apart from each other after some negotiation, and set it
out in the Separation Agreement they signed on 31 August 2016. This was despite the
fact that on 7 April 2016 (after the debacle of 24 February 2016), Andrew went to 725
Pritchard Farm Road and stayed until 10 April 2016. It was where and when Andrew
49

and the Plaintiff broke the news of their separation to Chris, and then later to Tara
telephonically.
178. As aforesaid, it is because of the Defendant’s claim for rectification, that I have
considered evidence extrinsic to the Separation Agreement, to make a determination on
whether Andrew and the Plaintiff separated much earlier than the 1 March 2016 date.
179. From the totality of the evidence, the extrinsic evidence led by the Defendant to
contradict the 1 March 2016 separation date, failed to support that case.
180. Being persuaded that the Plaintiff’s version of events: that it was her discovery of
Andrew’s infidelity on 24 February 2016 that moved them towards the end of their
common-law relationship, it is my view that the extrinsic evidence rather than negate
that date 1 March 2016 date, rather provided the background to the parties’ choice of it
as their separation date in the Separation Agreement they signed on 31 August 2016.
181. 1 March 2016, the date the parties agreed was their separation date, which is not
contradicted by the extrinsic evidence adduced in this case, is therefore the date the court
accepts as the date Andrew and Donna began their lives separate and apart from each
other, and not on any earlier date.
The Revocation Issue
182. As aforesaid, the Defendant does not challenge the validity of the 31 August 2016,
Separation Agreement entered into by Andrew and Donna. Her contention is that it
effectively revoked Andrew’s Will made in 2005 in which he appointed the Plaintiff his
executrix, and named her, the sole beneficiary thereof.
183.It is the Defendant’s case in this regard, that the Separation Agreement was intended to
and should conclude all matters between him and the Plaintiff, thus, excluding the
Plaintiff from benefitting from his estate despite the provisions of Andrew’s Will.
184. The Defendant relies on the release clauses in the Separation Agreement, in particular,
Clause 10 of the Separation Agreement which reads:
“ESTATE AND TESTAMENTARY DISPOSITION
10. The Parties renounce all rights each might have in or to the administration of the
other’s estate whether under any law of the Province of Manitoba , or any Province or
Territory of Canada, or any country in which any part of the estate of the other may be
50

situated and further waive and release the other from any and all rights of every kind,
nature, and description that each may acquire as a spouse or a surviving spouse in the
property assets or estate of the other”
177. Does Clause 10 of the Separation Agreement effectively revoke the devises made by
Andrew under the Will to the Plaintiff who is the sole beneficiary of the Will, thus
invalidating the Will?
Both experts: Mr. Pinsky KC and Ms. Walder, agreed in their reports, that the Separation
Agreement was badly drafted, and contained errors which led them to conclude that the
parties had not received independent legal advice. Even so, it is to be noted that the validity
of the Separation Agreement is not in question. What is in question is whether the parties
intended it to revoke the devises made to the Plaintiff under Andrew’s Will.
178. In determining whether the Separation Agreement effectively revoked Andrew’s Will
or was intended to achieve that end, I have regard to these pertinent issues:
1. Does the wording of the Separation Agreement revoke the Will?
2. if not, should extrinsic evidence be called in aid to determine an intention to exclude either
party from benefitting under their respective Wills?
3. Does extrinsic evidence establish an intent to exclude the Plaintiff from benefitting under
Andrew’s Will of which she is the named sole beneficiary?
The Wording:
179.The experts were asked separate questions in relation to the said issue regarding the
revocation of Andrew’s Will by the Separation Agreement.
Mr. Pinsky KC was asked:
“Does the Separation Agreement entered into between the late Mr. Andrew James
Clark and Ms. Donna Castellano on August 31, 2016, operate to revoke the Will
executed by Mr. Clark on April 15, 2005?
Ms. Walder was asked:
“Under Manitoba law, what is the effect on the validity of a Will where the Will
appoints a common-law partner of the testator as executor or trustee and/or makes a
devise or bequest of a beneficial interest to a common-law partner, the unregistered
51

common-law ends, and it is the subject of a Separation Agreement where the parties
agree to renounce their respective rights in the other’s estate?”
180.In their Joint Statement: they gave the following response:
“…We both agree that the separation agreement entered into between the deceased
and Ms. Castellano does not appear to revoke the Will executed by Mr. Clark. We
agree that according to the wording of the estate release clause in the separation
agreement and according to the law in Manitoba, the separation agreement is not
sufficient to oust the operation of the Will, as written. We also agree that the
separation agreement as it was drafted, executed, and exists does not contain a
renunciation of bequests or gifts pursuant to the Will. As it pertains to wills, bequests
and inheritances, there is only a renunciation of the right to administer the property”
181. Although Mr. Pinsky KC maintained his position during his cross-examination at
trial, Ms. Walder communicated her change of mind regarding the scope of Clause 10:
being their joint belief that Clause 10 related only to an agreement not to be involved
in the administration of each other’s estates. It was her view - expressed after she had
signed off on the Joint Statement - that Clause 10 excluded the Plaintiff from
benefiting from the estate, and therefore, that it revoked the devises to her under
Andrew’s Will.
182. I am inclined to agree with the position asserted by Mr. Pinsky KC having regard to
the glut of Manitoba case law which both experts agree, emphasises that the language
used in a Separation Agreement must be specific in order to revoke a Will.
183.Per Ms. Walder: “Generally, it appears clear that there must be highly specific
wording in a separation agreement that would lead to revocation or invalidation of a
will, with respect to the designation of a former common-law partner as executor or
trustee or a bequest to a former common-law partner. In the absence of such specific
wording, the express position of the Courts is that if a testator does not intend to
benefit a former common-law partner they are expected to make a new will.”
184. Mr. Pinsky KC agreed with this statement on how Manitoba Courts have dealt with
this matter in his report in which he stated: “Parties are usually careful in Separation
Agreements to try to the extent possible to extinguish any and all future claims where
their intention is to have finality. They commonly attempt to achieve this in Manitoba
52

by including lengthy and detailed releases. Although specific wording may vary, such
releases and waivers largely reflect the type of wording found in Ray-Ellis.”
185. The case law supports this. The point made in Ray-Ellis v. Goodtrack et al 3 In Ray-
Ellis, a Separation Agreement that was clear in the release of all claims, and under
which $100,000 was required to be paid to the ex-wife of Kirk Goodtrack, held sway
over a Locked in Retirement Account (“LIRA”) beneficiary designation. The release
was specific, and encompassing. IT read: “This release [ … ] is a full and final
settlement of all issues between Kirk Goodtrack and Soma Ray-Ellis and all rights
and obligations arising out of their relationship between the parties including their
marriage, separation and divorce. [ … ][ … ] Kirk Goodtrack and Soma Ray-Ellis
hereby release each other from all claims at common law, in equity or by statute
against each other, including but not limited to all claims under the Divorce Act, the
Family Law Act, and the Succession Law Reform Act (or any successor or similar
legislation whereby a spouse or former spouse is given a cause of action against his
or her spouse or the spouse’s estate for relief in the nature of support), for spousal
support of any kind, possession of property; ownership of property [ … ], division of
property, compensation for contributions to property, an equalization payment,
interest, penalties, and legal costs.
Kirk Goodtrack and Soma Ray-Ellis each hereby renounces any interest or entitlement
either may presently have, or that they may have in the future, in the other’s estate
upon the death of the other whether the other dies leaving a valid Will or dies intestate
without a valid Will. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing and for greater
certainty, Kirk Goodtrack and Soma Ray-Ellis agree that on the death of either party,
the surviving party shall not share in the deceased’s estate whether the deceased died
testate or intestate, nor shall the surviving spouse act as a personal representative of
the deceased’s estate, and the estate of the deceased party shall be distributed as
though the surviving party had died first.
Kirk Goodtrack and Soma Ray-Ellis hereby confirm that each remained financially
independent of the other following their separation and to this date, each continues
3 2021 ONSC 3102
53

to be financially independent of the other, and hereby releases his or her rights to
spousal support from the other forever. [ … ]
[ … ] [Kirk Goodtrack and Soma Ray-Ellis] wish to be allowed to get on with their
separate and independent lives, no matter what changes may occur. [ … ]
Kirk Goodtrack and Soma Ray-Ellis agree that the terms of the Agreement and this
Release are inextricably linked. [ … ]”
186.In juxtaposition with Clause 10 of the instant Separation Agreement, which is under
the head of ‘Estate and Testamentary Disposition’, it seems to me that unlike Ray-
Ellis, the language of Clause 10 is not specific enough as to make clear an intention
of the parties to exclude the other party from taking under a Will, and certainly, the
use of the vague terminologies: ‘surviving spouse’ and ‘estate’ or the heading ‘Estate
and Testamentary Disposition’ (which per the general provisions, were not intended
to be read as part of the Clause), could not cure this defect.
187. I am in no doubt that Clause 10, which seeks insulate the other party’s estate from
otherwise valid claims arising from the accrued rights of a common-law partner, as
framed, would effectively exclude the surviving party from involvement in the
administration of the other’s estate. However, it is my view that it did not specifically
exclude the other party: the Plaintiff, in this case, from participating in, or benefitting
from the estate in any other circumstance, not specified in the release contained in that
clause, such as a bequest or devise made to her under a Will.
188. I therefore find that the wording of Clause 10 lacks the necessary specificity in
relation to a Will, and is insufficient to oust Andrew’s intention to benefit the Plaintiff
under his validly executed Will, or to provide relief from a claim under the Will,
although it may do in relation to administration and claims in respect of rights
acquired under a common-law partnership.
189. It seems to me also, that the General Release in Clause 12 which is reproduced below,
does not take the matter further, as it is also general in nature, and incapable of
constituting the specific ouster of a Will validly executed. I reproduce it hereunder:
“GENERAL RELEASE
12. Each Party releases all claims whatsoever and however arising, whether under
the laws of the Province of Manitoba or any other jurisdiction, including and without
54

limiting the generality of the foregoing, whether arising by statute or at common law
and including actions founded on constrictive trust, resulting trust or unjust
enrichment, which such Party many now or hereafter have or acquire or be entitled
to against the property of the other Party however and whenever acquired, including
and without limiting the generality of the foregoing, real property, personal property,
any income from such property or assets of the other Party and any increase in the
value of such property, or against the estate of the other Party, but if any such action
should be brought the provisions of this Agreement may be pleaded as an answer to
any claim asserted and will constitute a full and complete defense thereto”
In my judgment, this provision, which is very general in nature, lends little to the
argument.
178. Having considered the provisions in the Separation Agreement, I cannot
help but agree with both experts that it was badly drafted, and Clause 10 is incapable
in its present wording of ousting Andrew’s intention to benefit the Plaintiff with his
estate upon his demise, expressed in a bequest (or devise) under his Will. As Mr.
Pinsky KC informed: “The release clause itself is neither robust nor fulsome by proper
Manitoba standards”.
190. Does the general release in Clause 12 which encompasses claims arising by statute
or at common law and actions founded on constructive trust, resulting trust or unjust
enrichment, have any significance as relates to the present proceedings?
191. I think not. The general release, wide as it appears to be, does not include in its
operation, bequests (or devises) made under a Will. The Plaintiff’s claim in these
proceedings, is not grounded on entitlement, legal or otherwise, to any part of
Andrew’s estate for any reason, including the fact of a common-law relationship with
its rights under Manitoba law. Nor does it seek enforcement of a right created by any
statute, or by the operation of the common-law. What it seeks is the enforcement of
provisions of a Will intended by Andrew to benefit her; a Will that was validly
executed in 2005 (there has been no challenge of its formal validity), and was not
revoked by any of the means set out in the Wills Act of Manitoba, including by
destruction, or replacement, even after they separated.
55

192. It is therefore a fact that the Separation Agreement does not as framed, revoke
Andrew’s Will.
Extrinsic Evidence
193. But is it possible that Andrew in fact intended by the Separation Agreement, to
deprive the Plaintiff of benefit in his estate, in effect revoking the Will he made in
2005, but the parties failed to express such an intent in their Separation Agreement?
That is the Defendant’s case, in pursuance of which she invites the court to look behind
the wording of the Separation Agreement to find an intention to revoke the Will.
194. In Mr. Pinsky KC’s report, adverting to the clarifying position espoused by the
Manitoba Court of Appeal in King v. Operating Engineers Training4 he provided
the following guidance on how Manitoba Courts have dealt with the matter of
extrinsic evidence in the construction of a written instrument such as a Separation
Agreement. He informed, that: “Where there is a Separation Agreement that is not
vague and does not require clarification, i.e., where it is clear and unequivocal, the
parole evidence rule operates in Manitoba …to prohibit extrinsic evidence respecting
the contract, especially where to do so would be to twist or alter the plain words
contained in the agreement or to permit only one side to present evidence of later
additional terms preferred by only one party in hindsight…”
“Having said that, where appropriate and not revisionist, the court may entertain
…contextual evidence of surrounding circumstances in order to understand prevalent
circumstances at the time where necessary, and only in circumstances where doing so
does not turn the actual terms of the agreement on their head…”
195.The nail upon which the Defendant hung her case that Andrew’s intention to revoke
the Will was not properly expressed in the Separation Agreement, was her allegation
that he did not have independent legal advice when he entered into the Separation
Agreement prepared by populating an internet template, so that the Separation
Agreement failed to set out the true intent of the parties, especially as there is
documentary evidence that Andrew in his emails, indicated that he and the Plaintiff
had been engaged in discussions on their estates.
4 2011 MBCA 80 (CanLII),
56

196.The failure of two persons without the benefit of legal advice, to adequately express
their intent in their written instrument, is the argument of Ms. Walder as she asserts
that a Manitoba Court may for that reason, go behind the Separation Agreement to
find the parties’ true intent and, to rectify same if it is found that the intent was not
properly expressed, for that reason.
197. To ascertain the intent of the parties in these circumstances, I consider it prudent to
examine the evidence led in this regard.
198. It is a matter not in controversy, that both Andrew and the Plaintiff dealt with lawyers
as they discussed the matter of how to deal with their asset sharing. This commenced
after the Plaintiff’s January 2016 enquiries about her pension entitlement, which
resulted in the couple having to fill out common-law relationship forms, including
pension release and homestead release forms. To do this, Alex Cudney for Donna, was
in touch with Terri Mestdagh for Andrew. The evidence of emails between Terri
Mestdagh and Andrew demonstrates that they were engaged in the discussion on the
end date of his relationship with Donna. Andrew wrote to Terri Mestdagh about his
position: that cohabitation ended in 2013 but he did also say that “I ended us in January
[2016]. Terri advised Andrew not to sign the pension release with the 1 March 2016
date Plaintiff was asking for.
199. It is obvious from the emails between herself and Andrew that Terri Mestdagh was a
trifle displeased with Andrew seeking her input without actually retaining her. Upon
her insistence, Andrew signed a Retainer Agreement and forwarded it to Terri
Mestdagh’s office on 27 June 2016. Whether Andrew also paid the retainer fee which
at the time was $2,500 is not disclosed by the evidence. What is disclosed, is that after
27 June 2016 when Andrew sent the completed Retainer Agreement, Terri Mestdagh
spoke with Alex Cudney on this matter of dates: end of cohabitation and the parties’
separation date, and thereafter, on 4 July 2016, the Plaintiff per an email agreed to use
the 1 July 2013 end of cohabitation date ‘for [Andrew’s] convenience’ and 1 March
2016 as the end of their relationship.
200. It was following these matters, that the Plaintiff on 6 July 2016, sent the lawdepot
online template of the Separation Agreement and suggested that they might use it,
which they did. There is evidence that there were back and forth negotiations between
57

Andrew and the Plaintiff regarding their separation, among the issues was the custody
of the dog Bozley. Eventually, each party indicated what agreement they would be
willing to sign.
201. Regarding the actual signing, Andrew engaged Mr. Tarrance De LaRonde for the
limited purpose of witnessing the signatures and the transmission/exchange of
documents. According to the Plaintiff this was brought to her attention, and her own
lawyer Alex Cudney informed her that he had also been working with her
administratively, as the parties to the Separation Agreement had negotiated it
themselves.
202.Having regard to these matters, should the court be inclined to go further to ascertain
the intention of the parties? It seems to me that that may be the prudent course to take.
203. The evidence regarding what the parties intended is not plentiful, but what is clear
from what is before the court, is that both Andrew and the Plaintiff used the word
‘estates’ in relation to their agreement. In his email of 23 June 2016, Andrew told Terri
Mestdagh: “the arrangement Donna and I had maintained was that neither of us
would be making claim against each other’s estates”. Also, in his email of 29 June
2016 once again to Terri Mestdagh, he stated: “The purpose of the SA to bilaterally
waive any and all claim to each other’s estate.” [my emphases] It is the evidence of
the Plaintiff, that they meant their ‘living estates’ as their discussions had been
concerned with determining their post-common-law separation rights to their
individual properties, and there had been no discussion of what was to happen to their
estates upon the demise of either party, or more specifically to their Wills.
204. It seems to me, having regard to the evidence, that such a curious use of the word
‘estate’ to refer to their assets which the Plaintiff asserts was the case, more probable
than not, and is supported by the evidence. I say for a number of reasons:
205. As voluminous as the documentary evidence relating to emails between Andrew and
Donna is, I have not found a scintilla of evidence relating to any discussion between
them by any means: email or text, or through their lawyers, on the subject of their
Wills, or even more generally, on either party’s estate upon death. On the evidence,
their discussions were concerned with their assets post separation, more particularly,
Andrew’s half share in the Plaintiff’s assets: her house (their homestead) and also her
58

pension, as Andrew had gained rights therein as a common-law partner in Manitoba.
Andrew had his company and his condominium in TCI and there is no evidence that
the Plaintiff made any claim for either. On the contrary, in her email to Andrew of 6
July 2016 she stated, “What’s yours is yours and what’s mine is mine… as we have
stated all along”.
206.It seems to me that despite Andrew’s use of the word ‘estate’ in two emails to Terri
Mestdagh, regarding the preparation of a Separation Agreement, there is no denying
that the context was his common-law rights in the Plaintiff’s pension and their
homestead and the releases the Plaintiff sought from him in their separation. Indeed,
that the context was with regard to assets sharing/protection, is evidenced by Terri
Mestdagh’s email to Andrew on 17 May 2016, in which she offered to prepare a
Separation Agreement. The subject was clearly on their joint assets: the Plaintiff’s
pension and her home (their homestead). I reproduce the relevant portion of the email:
“She’s really trying to do this backwards. I would never advise someone to waive
pension rights until I have seen the commuted value statement showing what it is that
you are giving up. Nor would I agree to have a client sign a waiver or homestead
release prior to signing a Separation agreement. If you are prepared to pay the cost
of having me to do so, I can prepare a Separation Agreement and you can sign it along
with the other documents …It is really up to you. She is the one who needs that pension
waiver so I am surprised that she is not prepared to have an Agreement prepared at
her cost.
Terri Mestdagh”
There does not appear to be any discussion regarding arrangements regarding his
estate upon death, including any mention of their existing Wills in this, or any other
discussion. That Andrew understood the bent of their discussions is revealed in his
email of 4 July 2016, when apparently frustrated with the Plaintiff, he threatened that
her insistence on using 1 March 2016 as the end of relationship date would place her
‘in a precarious position with assets exposed’.
207. It must be noted that these references to ‘estate’ made by Andrew, were made to his
own lawyer Terri Mestdagh, not to the Plaintiff, with whom he was engaged in
negotiations regarding their separation. There is also no record that Terri Mestdagh in
59

her communication with Alex Cudney who was representing the Plaintiff, made
mention of estate matters in their discussions. It is therefore doubtful that Andrew’s
use of the word ‘estate’ would be evidence of an agreement regarding what was to
happen to a party’s estate upon the demise of the other, especially as they had executed
Wills in 2005.
208. It seems to me, furthermore, that Andrew’s statement to Terri Mestdagh: “The
purpose of the SA is to bilaterally waive any and all claim to each other’s estate,”
would be incongruous with the Separation Agreement which was actually signed if
the use of the word ‘estate’ was in relation to their estate upon death (properly so
called) rather than the ‘living estates’ that the Plaintiff alleged was their
understanding. After all, of the twenty-five clauses in the Separation Agreement, only
one clause (Clause 10) dealt with estate matters, and without a mention of their Wills
at that. It would be surprising and certainly out of character for Andrew (who from
the evidence was shrewd regarding the use of his money), if dealing with their estates
upon death was all they needed a Separation Agreement for, to not revoke his Will,
without having to retain counsel to prepare a Separation Agreement for upward of
$2,500. Or, having gone through the process of making such Agreement with the
Plaintiff, to have neglected to make mention of their Wills if he intended the
Separation Agreement to revoke his Will.
209. I am persuaded that Andrew’s use of the word ‘estates’ was a misnomer, intended to
refer to their assets post separation, rather than their estates upon death, as the
Defendant canvasses as her argument.
210. Nor, am I persuaded by the Defendant’s argument that the 2005 Wills having been
entered into by both Andrew and the Plaintiff, on the advice of the Financial Advisor
would somehow make it necessary to revoke it by a Separation Agreement. That
argument is difficult to follow or accept, for, as I have previously observed, there is
not a single email or other communication in which the individual Wills of Andrew
and the Plaintiff were mentioned throughout the process of their separation. This fact,
in my view, is the strongest argument against what is alleged, that Clause 10 was
intended to also exclude the operation of the Wills the parties knew they had executed
in each other’s favour even though there was no specific mention of them.
60

211.I find it instructive, that the Plaintiff after the signing of the Separation Agreement
executed a new Will which excluded Andrew who had been a beneficiary along with
her children under the Will she also made in 2005, at the same time as Andrew did.
The Plaintiff’s attention to changing her Will would seem to indicate that she did not
understand that the Separation Agreement addressed what was to happen to her estate
upon death in relation to her existing Will.
212. Although Andrew like the Plaintiff had no legal training, Andrew was by all accounts
a very intelligent man. He had available to him legal advice if he so wanted, for he
had signed a Retainer Agreement with Terri Mestdagh after having had the benefit of
her advice regarding the signing of the pension and homestead waiver which was part
of the discussion on his separation from the Plaintiff. Andrew gave Ms. Mestdagh
instructions regarding the end of his cohabitation with the Plaintiff, alleging that he
had not lived in Canada since 1 July 2013. This was a matter Terri Mestdagh
communicated to Alex Cudney on Andrew’s behalf. Whether Andrew sought Terri
Mestdagh’s view on any other matter, including the content of the Separation
Agreement he negotiated it with the Plaintiff, is not evidence before this court. I
however have no reason to conclude (having regard to the signed Retainer
Agreement), that he did not do so, at all, or that he did not know what he was doing
when he negotiated the Agreement with the Plaintiff.
213. I am persuaded of the latter point, for having had some advice from Terri Mestdagh
in relation to the end of relationship arrangements, and having in fact received her
advice not to sign the Pension Release with a 1 March 2016 date, it seems to me more
probable than not, that he did get advice from her in relation to the Separation
Agreement. However, if he did not, that would mean that he made a decision to
dispense with the lawyer’s services as he negotiated the Separation Agreement. On
this possibility, I refer to Andrew’s email of 17 May 2016, which was in response to
an email from Terri Mestdagh offering to prepare a Separation Agreement for him, he
stated: “Can you provide an estimate of cost? Depending on the costs I may just
proceed on my own.”
214. Thus, if indeed, Andrew decided to dispense with the services of the lawyer he had
retained, it must have been because he believed himself to be quite capable of settling
61

his affairs with the Plaintiff, and not that he was deprived in any way, or for any reason
from having access to independent legal advice. As a party to the Separation
Agreement, he must in such a circumstance, be held to the bargain he entered into
with his eyes open.
215. But I am persuaded that Andrew knew what he was doing and understood the process.
I am therefore satisfied that the Separation Agreement did record what the parties
actually discussed and intended to govern their relationship post separation, and it was
not intended to affect the Will Andrew made in favour of the Plaintiff, which he could
have, but did not, revoke.
216. On the evidence, it was in 2018 - two years after the Separation Agreement - that
Andrew now with a fiancée Rebecca Strohl, communicated his intention to make a
new Will to benefit his fiancée, her mother, and his mother (the Defendant).
Unfortunately, he did not complete the process before his death.
217. I therefore find that even looking behind the Separation Agreement, it is not clear
whether Andrew had independent legal advice or did not, for he did have a lawyer
whom he had retained formally by signing a Retainer Agreement which he had
delivered to her on 27 June 2016.
218. I also find that there is no evidence to even alert one that Andrew, by many accounts,
very intelligent, and shrewd, did not understand what was released and what was not,
in the Separation Agreement. He in fact went through the draft, and rejected the
provisions that included giving the Plaintiff time with Bozley. He thereafter, indicated
to the Plaintiff what Agreement he was willing to sign.
219. I further find from the evidence that what Andrew and the Plaintiff had within their
contemplation as they negotiated their Separation Agreement, was with regard to their
individual asset entitlement in their separation as a common-law couple. I am in no
doubt that in this regard, they intended to settle all matters between them with finality.
That this was Andrew’s state of mind at the time the Separation Agreement was
signed, is the evidence of Robert who introduced Andrew to Terrance de LaRonde and
was at this time quite involved in Andrew’s life and therefore appeared to be credible.
220. Thus did the Separation Agreement deal with the Property, Dower (Homestead) and
Pension Releases, as well as a General Release, and in Clause 5 of the Separation
62

Agreement stated that: "The parties acknowledge that they have agreed upon a
division of all assets owned or possessed by them as common-law and marital [sic]
property or separate property. The parties are in possession of all of those assets to
which each is respectively entitled. Accordingly, neither party makes any claim to any
assets in the possession of the other."
221. Having said this, I must reiterate that the evidence does not disclose an intention to
deprive the Plaintiff of Andrew’s bequests under his Will, although Clause 10 of their
Separation Agreement, would appear to exclude her from being involved in the
administration of his estate upon his demise, and from being able to make any claims
on his estate by reason of their common-law relationship and the rights pertaining to
that estate, but not her participation or her benefitting in his estate in any other
circumstance, including per a bequest (or devise) in a Will, see: Pearson v Pearson
Estate5.
222. In that case which appears to be on all fours with the instant matter, a son sued his
mother the named sole executrix and beneficiary of the Will of her husband claiming
one-third of the net value of the estate. The mother, in her personal capacity, executed
a release or quit claim during their separation on February in which, in consideration
of the payment to her of $10,000.00, she gave up all her present and future interest in
the estate of her husband. It was contended that for that reason, he had died intestate
and the three children were the persons entitled to the entire estate. thee other two
children were not parties to the suit.
223.The release read:
“I, MARION PEARSON, of the City of Calgary, inn Alberta, wife of CARL ROBERT
PEARSON, of the Rural Municipality of Odanah, in Manitoba, Farmer, do hereby, in
consideration of the sum of $1.00 One Dollar now paid to me by the said CARL
ROBERT PEARSON (the receipt whereof is hereby acknowledged) and the payment
to me of the sum of Ten Thousand ($10,000.00) Dollars from the proceeds of the sale
of the following lands, namely: by the said CARL ROBERT PEARSON Do By These
Presents release, discharge, and quit claim the said CARL ROBERTPEARSON, his
heirs, executors, administrators and assigns, from, of, and on account of, any claim,
5 1980 MBCA 57
63

in law or in equity, past, present, or future which I may have heretofore had, or now,
or in future may have, against the said CARL ROBERT PEARSON or his estate, under
and by virtue of the laws of Manitoba or any other province of Canada, under The
Devolution of Estates Act, The Wills Act, The Testator's Family Maintenance Act, The
Dower Act, or any other enactment whatsoever. This release shall be binding upon
me, the said MARION PEARSON and nay heirs, executors, administrators and
assigns.
DATED at the City of Calgary, in the Province of Alberta, this 28 day of February,
A.D. 1978.”
The Court of Appeal of Manitoba per Hall JA held “…The issue thus arises of whether
Mrs. Pearson, by the terms of the release or quit claim, is bound to forego any benefit
under her husband's will. In approaching that matter, it is important to recognize that
she is not advancing a claim under the will or any relevant statute of Manitoba; rather
the son, Roderick, is endeavouring to prevent her from accepting the benefit under a
valid will. With respect, I am unable to construe the release as meaning that Mrs.
Pearson agreed to forego any benefit which her husband might choose to confer upon
her by will, whether such will was made before or after the making of the release. At
most, the release or quit claim forecloses any claim from her independent of the will.
8 In short, the son Roderick seeks to prevent her from accepting the benefits conferred
under the will, and I am unable to construe the release as amounting to an obligation
on her part to forego those benefits.”
I am persuaded that in this case also, I find that Clause 10 even read together with
Clause 12 of the Separation Agreement, could not deprive the Plaintiff of the benefit
conferred to her by Andrew’s Will.
The Rectification Issue
224.Should the Separation Agreement be rectified?
At the risk of tedium, I rehearse that on 15 April 2005, Andrew executed a Will in
which he appointed the Plaintiff whom he described as his fiancé [sic], his executrix
and sole beneficiary. On 31 August 2016, they executed a Separation Agreement
which they stated was “intended to settle the matters addressed”. They also stated
64

therein that their separation (living separate and apart from each other), commenced
on 1 March 2016.
225. In support of her counterclaim for rectification, the Defendant in summary, argues,
that the Separation Agreement was intended to deal with all matters between Andrew
and the Plaintiff who had permanently terminated their relationship with finality,
Thus, she urges that any reading of the Separation Agreement which would suggest
that this was not so was ‘genuinely shocking and contrary to utmost good faith and
fair dealing’. In her submission, the only way to achieve what is “just and equitable”
and which will “include a comprehensive renunciation of the Plaintiff and Deceased’s
rights into the estate of the other whether under a will (or the Will) or otherwise.” is
to permit the rectification of the Separation Agreement.
226. What does the Defendant require the court to do on the first of these particulars? Is it
to alter the language in the release Clauses, in particular Clause 10, which both experts
have said is incapable of excluding the bequests made to the Plaintiff under the Will
for lack of specificity, or the general release in Clause 12? Is it to include the heading
of Clause 10 which the Separation Agreement was not intended to be read with it? Is
it to include words that exclude the participation and benefit under the will of the
surviving partner, in the sub-paragraph that excludes administration and claims under
statute or common-law of a common-law partnership?
227. The law on rectification in Manitoba , is, as both experts agree, not intended to correct
errors, but simply to correct a mistake in the written instrument. The nature of the
mistake which may be rectified, should be: that although the parties were in agreement
as to the terms, the recording of their agreement contained an error which would not
reflect the agreement of the parties. The leading case on this in Manitoba: Canada
(Attorney General) v. Fairmont Hotels Inc.,6 agrees with this statement of the law.
In that case, the Supreme Court of Canada set out what may be granted by way of
rectification: “Rectification requires both parties to agree that there was a mistake in
most cases. It is not intended to alter terms originally agreed upon where a party or
even both Parties believe it prudent or beneficial to do so. Rather it is intended to
6 [2016] 2 SCR 720
65

correct or rectify a document that erroneously did not reflect the parties’ true
intentions at the time the agreement was reached” [my emphasis]
Fairmont also helpfully provides this guidance regarding how the rectification may
be achieved: “It therefore falls to a party seeking rectification to show not only the
putative error in the instrument, but also the way in which the instrument should be
rectified in order to correctly record what the parties intended to do. The court’s task
in a rectification is corrective, not speculative….Where therefore an instrument
recording an agreed-upon course of action is sought to be rectified, the party seeking
rectification must identify terms which were omitted or recorded incorrectly and which
correctly recorded are sufficiently precise to constitute the terms of an enforceable
agreement”
228. The Defendant, without seeking to invalidate the Separation Agreement, has raised
two issues for which she seeks its rectification in these particulars:
1. rectification which will terminate all rights of the Plaintiff under the Will;
2. rectification which will correctly state the date of Separation being a date
significantly earlier than three years prior to the death of the Deceased.
229. The question this raises is this: even if the claim for rectification were clear in what
was sought, would such rectification reflect what the parties had agreed upon, so that
the court’s function would simply be corrective?
230. It is my view that the instant case is not one for which rectification can be called in
aid or is even called for, for the evidence does not support the Defendant’s contention.
Ms. Walder in the Joint Report, stated: ““In order for the principle of rectification to
apply, there must be a mutual mistake on the part of the parties that must be
rectified…If all the evidence at trial indicates that it was the common mistake of both
parties to release any and all claims to the other’s estate including any Wills
previously made by either party, but by mutual mistake, the estate clause in the
agreement fails to contain the required specific wording or reference to existing Wills,
this portion of the separation agreement could be rectified to include the mention of
the existing Wills. ””
I rehearse in summary, the evidence led by the Defendant in respect of what she
alleges to be Andrew’s intent to exclude the Plaintiff from his estate, including in
66

taking under his Will, as well as evidence of a date significantly earlier than the 1
March 2016 date.
Regarding the rectification sought to terminate any interest the Plaintiff may have in
Andrew’s estate, there is simply no evidence from which such intent may be
discerned. It seems to me that what the Defendant relies on is an emotive argument
(or plea) regarding what she says would result in injustice to her, the mother of
Andrew, should the Plaintiff from whom he had separated be entitled to the benefit of
his estate. She contends, that Andrew could not have intended that having separated
from the Plaintiff and settled his affairs away from her, that she should benefit from
his estate.
231. It seems to me that without cogent evidence that the parties intended to address the
matter of their Wills, but by mistake left it out of their Agreement, the Defendant’s
argument in this regard, is an invitation for this court to indulge in a speculative
exercise as to what the parties (or Andrew) intended on 31 August 2016 regarding to
what he had set out in his Will in 2005 which he had not revoked.
232. I note that one of the areas of disagreement Ms. Walder expressed in the experts’ Joint
report was the question of intention and how the courts deal with instruments executed
without the benefit of independent legal advice. I note her comment, citing the
minority view in Pearson, that: “If ignorance or inadvertence led to the failure of the
testator to revoke his will, it is arguable that equity could operate so as not to permit
such ignorance or inadvertence to be defined as intention on the part of the testator
to benefit the plaintiff with his entire estate.”
233. I have already observed that there is no evidence regarding what the parties intended
in respect of their estates as it related to the Wills they had made, as that did not form
part of their discussions during their negotiation of the Separation Agreement.
234. It must be noted, that in 2005 when Andrew made his Will, he did not mention the
Defendant as a beneficiary at all. Even Robert (Bob) Clark, Andrew’s father (with
whom, by many accounts, he shared a close relationship), had a benefit under
Andrew’s Will which was subordinated to the Plaintiff who had been in a seven-year
common-law relationship with Andrew at the time.
67

235. On the evidence, Andrew had a family relationship not only with the Plaintiff, but
with her children and grandchildren in whose life he participated fully as stepfather
and grandfather. In this regard, he acknowledged in his email to a third party (Olga of
The Atrium) a father-daughter relationship with Tara; he was present in the life of
Chris and participated in his wedding. Kathryn Payne spoke of how she observed both
Andrew and Donna relate to the grandchildren. His special relationship with Cobain
was acknowledged and he communicated his desire to take Cobain and Pepper when
they turned eight and five years to Disney World.
236. On the other hand, on the Defendant’s own showing, Andrew drew closer in
relationship with her and brother Robert, only after he separated from the Plaintiff.
237. Fairmont clarified: “The court’s task in a rectification is corrective, not speculative”.
Would it not be speculative, in the light of the relationship Andrew had with Donna
and her family (which was acknowledged even in the funeral brochures of their
respective families), on one side, and his relationship with the Defendant in those
years, in the absence of any mention of their Wills at a time when they were
negotiating their separation, for this court to assume, that because Andrew was
separating from the Plaintiff, he did not want her (and through her, any of these
persons who had presented as his immediate family for eighteen years), to benefit
from his estate?
Could the court indeed rule out the possibility that Andrew, knowing that he had a
Will, could have had a plan in 2016, to give himself time to consider whether he
wanted to retain the Plaintiff as his beneficiary, or to change his Will when he was
ready, hence the absence of its mention in the Separation Agreement?
238. There is no answer to these questions which would exclude surmise, for as has been
said previously, there is no evidence that Andrew and Donna discussed their Wills at
all, as they negotiated their Separation Agreement, yet the evidence shows that
Andrew was not oblivious of estate matters, for in 2018, when he had a fiancée; he
decided to make a new Will to benefit his fiancée, her mother, and the Defendant with
whom he had (from her own, and Rebecca Strohl’s account) developed a closer
relationship at the time, such that she visited with him in TCI in that year. That
Andrew’s relationship with the Defendant in 2018, was markedly different from their
68

2005 relationship is demonstrated by the fact that in 2005 when he made his first Will,
he made no mention of the Defendant all.
239. The only circumstance upon which the Defendant relies to make the case for
rectification is the fact that Andrew and Donna separated, and dealt with their assets
in a Separation Agreement. It bears repetition that the Separation Agreement made no
mention of their Wills. The Plaintiff, a party to the Separation Agreement swore to
this in her affidavit before the Manitoba Court: “Based on legal advice I was of the
understanding that Clause 10 related to intestacy or administration of the other’s
estate in the event of intestacy; however, should we elect not to change our Wills or
similarly change beneficiary designations on insurance policies then the person so
designated would still be entitled to any such benefit.”
240. The Plaintiff made a new Will after the Separation agreement was signed. That act
would support what she claims was her understanding: that the Separation Agreement
did not revoke the Will.
241. Thus, even if Andrew had intended that their Wills be revoked by the Separation
Agreement (and there is no such evidence), the Plaintiff’s act, and her explanation of
it would mean that they would not have been ad idem on that score. The evidence
would therefore not support a meeting of minds not correctly recorded - the
circumstance under which rectification maybe granted, as settled by the Canadian
Supreme Court in Fairmont.
242. In any event, the Defendant’s counterclaim for rectification fails to clearly set out
what is sought which would of itself, argue against the court granting a claim which
has no certainty regarding the claimant’s prayer.
243.What is the court required to do when it is asked to rectify the Separation Agreement
“…so as to terminate all rights of the Plaintiff under the Will”?
I repeat for my guidance what the court said in Fairmont: “It therefore falls to a party
seeking rectification to show not only the putative error in the instrument, but also the
way in which the instrument should be rectified in order to correctly record what the
parties intended to do…”
244. It seems to me that short of asking this court to rewrite the Separation Agreement for
the parties when one of the parties is unavailable, it is not clear what the Defendant
69

seeks by way of rectification in that regard. I can only speculate that perhaps, the court
is being invited to insert the mention of the parties’ Wills into Clause 10, when there
is no evidence that such a discussion took place during the negotiation that culminated
in the parties’ Separation Agreement.
245. In my view, unless there is cogent evidence from which the court can say “Aha! how
did they in their writing, miss an intent so clearly expressed by both parties regarding
what was to become of their Wills”, any attempt to rectify the Separation Agreement
to read their Wills into it, or some other language to revoke the Will of Andrew, would
be an enterprise shaped by speculation and not an expression of the true intent of the
parties in what was expressed by a provision in the Separation Agreement, to be the
parties’ entire Agreement.
246.I daresay that such is not the purpose of rectification, even if the Defendant had
specified to the court what matter should be rectified, and in what manner.
247. The same may be said of the rectification sought in respect of the date. Regarding
this, I have previously found, having gone through the evidence, that the evidence
supports the 1 March 2016 date that the parties agreed was the date they lived separate
and apart from each other, rather than the 1 July 2013, or any other date earlier than
what the parties set out in their Separation Agreement.
248. This court cannot therefore grant the rectification of the Separation Agreement the
Defendant seeks on her counterclaim.
The Laches Issue:
249. The Defendant further raises a defence of probate laches to the Plaintiff’s claim,
pointing to two matters: that the Plaintiff failed to lodge a caveat to the Defendant’s
petition for Letters of Administration in respect of Andrew’s estate in January 2019
which resulted in a grant of representation to the Defendant. She alleges that the
Plaintiff had had notice of her petition but had neglected to challenge it; and also, that
having begun the instant proceedings, she failed to seek the surrender or the
revocation of the Letters of Administration. She contends that this is conduct on which
she placed reliance. Thus, she canvasses, to permit the Plaintiff’s suit to continue,
70

would be to work an injustice to her, especially as the Plaintiff seeks equitable
compensation in relation to her administration of Andrew’s estate.
Would the application of the equitable doctrine of laches (or probate laches), operate
to estop the Plaintiff from making her instant claims, as the Defendant contends?
I think not, for the reasons I provide:
250. In consideration of this issue, it is important to set in context, the matters upon which
the defence is asserted:
As earlier recounted, in the immediate aftermath of Andrew’s death, the Plaintiff and
the Defendant spoke amicably. The Plaintiff in fact offered help to the Defendant
which consisted of providing the Defendant with information on TCI, where the
Defendant was due to travel in relation to the death of Andrew, as well as information
on the address of the lawyer who might be in possession of Andrew’s Will.
I have not found clear evidence (apart from the hearsay evidence in that regard in the
evidence of Kathryn Payne) as to how or why their relationship soured, but on 8
August 2018, the Defendant commenced pre-emptive proceedings in the Manitoba
Court, against any petition the Plaintiff might bring for the grant of probate of
Andrew’s Will.
251. On 6 December 2018, the Plaintiff did apply for the grant of probate by the Manitoba
Court. The Defendant, having received notice of this, challenged the Plaintiff’s
petition. To deal with the contentious probate matter in which an issue of Andrew’s
domicile was raised, the Manitoba Court dealt with this conflict of laws issue, as a
preliminary matter, to determine the forum conveniens for the trial of the matters
related to Andrew’s estate. The Plaintiff and the Defendant herein, were parties to the
said proceedings. Yet, on 25 January 2019, while the said proceedings were pending
in Manitoba, the Defendant petitioned for the grant of Letters of Administration from
this court: the Supreme Court of TCI. The basis of her petition was that Andrew had
died intestate, his existing Will having (as she alleged), been revoked by a Separation
Agreement he entered into with the Plaintiff before his death.
252. It is the case of the Defendant that notice of her petition for Letters of Administration
at the TCI Court was filed along with her papers before the Manitoba Court, so that
the Plaintiff had notice of it. Furthermore, as notice of her petition was published in
71

the TCI Gazette, the Plaintiff who had lawyers in TCI, must have known about it.
Despite this, the Plaintiff failed to file a caveat to her petition.
253. Following the grant to the Defendant, she commenced administration on the strength
of the Letters, even though the Manitoba Court continued with the proceedings to
determine the question of the forum conveniens for the trial of the contentious probate
matter. That question was settled in favour of the TCI Court, where these proceedings
had advanced.
254. In the instant suit the Plaintiff at first made no claim for a revocation of the Letters of
Administration which had been granted to the Defendant by this Court, or the
surrender of same. It was not until evidence closed, that the Plaintiff brought an
amendment (which was granted) to include a claim for revocation of the grant of the
Letters of Administration.
255. It is the argument of the Defendant citing in the main, the case of James v.
Scudamore,7 that the failure of the Plaintiff to file a caveat to the Defendant’s
application amounted to acquiescence to the grant to the Defendant, and she must be
held to be bound by the result.
The Defendant argues also that the grant of the Letters of Administration to her in any
event, was res judicata on the question of the entitlement to the administration of
Andrew’s estate, and therefore, inconsistent with the Plaintiff’s instant claims.
The Defendant further contends that she has relied on the Plaintiff’s failure to
challenge the Letters, or seek revocation thereof, to administer the estate without
affording her opportunity for protective relief. In her submission, this opens her up to
claims relating to the administration of the estate: such as the claim for equitable
compensation herein for maladministration.
256. In consequence of these matters, the Defendant asserts the defence of probate laches,
contending that to enable the Plaintiff to pursue her claims in these circumstances,
would be to work an injustice to her.
257. In considering the asserted defence, I have regard to Scudamore cited for my
guidance. The problem of the claimant in Scudamore was that he waited an
inordinately long period (over 20 years) before challenging a 2002 codicil to his
7 EWHC 996 (Ch)
72

father’s 1998 will. During that time, he took no steps to contest the Will, and even
though he had earlier sought legal advice, only brought proceedings after the estate
had long been administered. The court having found his delay in bringing the
proceedings, unjustified, unreasonable, and prejudicial to the defendants, as the estate
had been administered, barred his claim. The court in its judgment, after a review of
a great number of cases, helpfully set out the key factors for a court to consider where
laches (or probate laches) is asserted. These factors, which were set out as propositions
are, that:“(1) Where a person having a right to intervene in existing probate
proceedings is aware of those proceedings and of that right, but deliberately abstains
from joining in them, he or she is bound by the result…
(2) Explicable delay, even when coupled with taking a legacy under a will proved in
common form, is not generally enough to bar a claimant from taking probate
proceedings…
(3) But unjustified delay, possibly on its own… and certainly when coupled with acts
amounting to waiver of the claimant’s right, will bar the claim…
(4) Similarly where the delay has led to others’ detrimental reliance on the inaction,
such as distribution of the estate.
258. Is the defence of laches asserted by the Defendant tenable?
In my judgment, the facts of this case must be distinguished from Scudamore, and the
line of cases relied on therein. This is because of the peculiar facts of this case which
differentiate the Plaintiff’s conduct from the conduct held out as constituting laches
(or probate laches).
I have already recounted how it was that the Plaintiff’s application for the grant of
probate became transformed into proceedings in Manitoba to determine the forum
conveniens for the trial of what had become a contentious probate matter. I have also
recounted that the Letters of Administration were applied for by the Defendant from
this court, while that important matter was pending before that Court.
259. It seems to me that unlike Scudamore, the Plaintiff, far from sleeping on her rights,
had effectively asserted her claim as a beneficiary in the estate of Andrew, and sought
to prove his Will. This was before the Defendant, who being a party to those
proceedings, went behind the Manitoba Court which was seised with the matter and
73

was determining a preliminary issue, to petition this court for the Letters. That the
Plaintiff failed to lodge a caveat in these circumstances does not in my view,
demonstrate her acquiescence to this court’s grant of representation to the Defendant.
Indeed, rather than lodge a caveat against the grant, the Plaintiff began the present
proceedings in 2019 after the fact, seeking in the main, to prove the Will of Andrew
and her entitlement to the bequests therein.
260. Were the facts of this case such as did not include a Will, probate of which was being
sought by the Plaintiff as executrix and beneficiary, a probate action that required the
Manitoba Court to determine the forum conveniens for trial, and Letters of
Administration granted on a premise that was false to the knowledge of the petitioner,
as it was contested and yet to be determined, perhaps the Defendant’s insistence that
the grant she holds from this Court which the Plaintiff did not try to stop by caveat,
should entitle her to continue in that office, might have had some merit. It is however
not so.
261. Should the Plaintiff have participated in the petition of the Defendant for Letters of
Administration under a false or wrong premise? I strongly opine that she should have
if she had had notice of it. Yet her failure to respond to what she may justifiably have
perceived to be the Defendant’s attempt to overreach her claim in the Manitoba
proceedings by applying for representation on an unproven assertion, would scarcely,
in the peculiar circumstances of this case, be evidence of acquiescence, indolence, or
tardiness in asserting her right over the estate - conduct for which on the application
of the doctrine of laches (or probate laches), she should be held to be bound by the
Letters of Administration granted to the Defendant.
262.The procedure adopted by the Plaintiff was convoluted, and perhaps, ill-advised, for
the lodgment of a caveat would have settled the matter, and provided this court with
the opportunity to look closely into the claim of intestacy asserted by the Defendant
in her petition. If the Plaintiff had had notice of the Defendant’s petition to this Court,
which was based on a premise that was a live issue in the Manitoba Court and for
which that Court was in the process of determining the preliminary matter of the forum
conveniens for the trial, then it was unfortunate that the Plaintiff deprived this court
of that opportunity to do its work with adequate information.
74

263. But a wrong procedure was not a right unasserted, for the Defendant was never at any
time, after 6 December 2018 when the Plaintiff applied for probate of Andrew’s Will
(which was before she made her petition for the Letters), in doubt as to the Plaintiff’s
asserted claim as a beneficiary of Andrew’s Will.
264. Such conduct is not what laches (or probate laches) is concerned with. I therefore on
these peculiar facts, do not find merit in the asserted defence, as I am persuaded that
the doctrine of probate laches is inapplicable to this case with the recounted peculiar
facts, of a Plaintiff robustly pursuing her claim, unlike the claimant in Scudamore and
other similar cases on the subject of probate laches. I am guided in this, by the
clarifying dictum of Selborne LC in Lindsay Petroleum Co v Hurd:8 “Now the
doctrine of laches in Courts of Equity is not an arbitrary or a technical doctrine.
Where it would be practically unjust to give a remedy, either because the party has,
by his conduct, done that which might fairly be regarded as equivalent to a waiver of
it, or where by his conduct and neglect he has, though perhaps not waiving that
remedy, yet put the other party in a situation in which it would not be reasonable to
place him if the remedy were afterwards to be asserted, in either of these cases, lapse
of time and delay are most material. But in every case, if an argument against relief,
which otherwise would be just, is founded upon mere delay, that delay of course not
amounting to a bar by any statute of limitations, the validity of that defence must be
tried upon principles substantially equitable. Two circumstances, always important in
such cases, are, the length of the delay and the nature of the acts done during the
interval, which might affect either party and cause a balance of justice or injustice in
taking the one course or the other, so far as relates to the remedy.”
265. Should this court close the door to the Plaintiff’s claim relating to Andrew’s Will and
her interest in it, for non-participation in the Defendant’s petition brought by her in
TCI alleging intestacy during the pendency of the contentious proceedings to
determine the validity of Andrew’s Will?
266. I think not, in the circumstances of this case. Were this court to hold otherwise, it
would be condoning the Defendant’s action and thereby, enabling her to profit from
her own wrong of going behind the Plaintiff’s claims before the Manitoba Court by
8 (1874) LR 5 PC 221 at 239-40
75

securing the administration of the estate on the premise of Andrew’s intestacy, with
full knowledge that the Plaintiff’s claim asserted otherwise, in the Manitoba
proceedings which were pending at the material time.
267. The Plaintiff amended her pleading to assert that the Letters of Administration were
wrongly issued on erroneous information. She also amended her claim to include a
claim for revocation of the Letters of Administration granted by this Court to the
Defendant.
268. The Defendant’s argument that the said amendment introduced a new cause of action
that transformed the suit into a probate action, leaving the Defendant with the
consequence of being subject to a claim for compensation for her administration of
the estate, did not find favour with this Court which held that not only was it not a
new cause of action, but that it was a necessary consequential relief that would come
into play if the Plaintiff was to succeed in her claim, for if the Will was held to be
valid, that circumstance would be inconsistent with the Defendant’s continued holding
of the Letters of Administration granted on a contested, unproven premise.
269. I see in these facts, nothing resembling the inaction of the complainant in Scudamore,
for which the Plaintiff’s claim should be disallowed on the ground probate laches. The
Defendant’s asserted defence of probate laches must therefore fail.
270. Nor am I persuaded by the Defendant’s argument that the grant of Letters of
Administration without the Plaintiff’s challenge establishes res judicata.
271. It is trite learning, that a grant or representation in common form is revocable, and in
this instance that it was obtained on an unproven premise, it would be subject to
revocation if the premise was proven to be false.
272. Has the Plaintiff’s claim been made out?
It seems to me that it has, for the evidence supports a finding that Andrew’s Will which
was executed in 2005 in favour of the Plaintiff which has not been contested for formal
validity, remains valid and unrevoked either by the Separation Agreement, or by the
effluxion of time, being that Andrew and Donna separated less than three years before
the death of Andrew. The bequests to the Plaintiff who Andrew appointed sole
executrix are therefore not revoked under section 18(4)(e) of the Wills Act of
Manitoba which reads:
76

“18(4) Where in a will
(a) a devise or bequest of a beneficial interest in property is made to the common-law
partner of the testator;
(b) the common-law partner of the testator is appointed executor or trustee; or
(c) a general or special power of appointment is conferred on a common-law partner
of the testator; and after making the will and before the death of the testator, the
testator's common-law relationship with their common-law partner is terminated
(d) …
(e) where the common-law relationship was not registered under section 13.1 of The
Vital Statistics Act, by virtue of having lived separate and apart for a period of at
least three years; then, unless a contrary intention appears in the will, the devise,
bequest, appointment or power is revoked and the will shall be construed as if the
common-law partner predeceased the testator.” [emphasis supplied].
273.The Plaintiff has relinquished her claim for equitable compensation relating to the
Defendant’s administration on the strength of the Letters of Administration obtained
under the unproven premise of Andrew’s intestacy.
As no evidence was led with regard to wasting of the estate, or maladministration, the
abandonment of that claim is not without justification.
274. Has the Defendant’s counterclaim been made out?
No, it has not. In her counterclaim. the Defendant has sought for a declaration inter
alia, that she is the lawfully appointed sole personal representative of the estate of
Andrew James Clark and that the Plaintiff has no right or entitlement to the
administration of his estate; that the caution registered under instrument number
#3059/19 on the register for parcel 60804/157/K44, The Bight and Thomas Stubbs,
Providenciales was wrongfully made.
She seeks consequential orders for the removal of the caution, an injunction to restrain
the Plaintiff from interfering with the Defendant’s administration of Andrew’s estate,
damages, interest on the damages and a claim for the rectification of the Separation
Agreement, so as to terminate all of Donna’s rights under the Will, and to correctly
state the date of Separation, being a date significantly earlier than three years prior to
the death of the Deceased.
77

In the light of the finding that Andrew’s Will in 2005 is not revoked by the Separation
Agreement; that the rectification sought by the Defendant “…so as to terminate all
rights of the Plaintiff under the Will and to correctly state the date of Separation being
a date significantly earlier than three years prior to the death of the Deceased” is
without merit and will not be granted; that the Plaintiff and Andrew were in a
common-law relationship which ended on 1 March 2016, less than three years before
his 9 July 2018 death, and therefore outside the operation of 18(4)(e) of the Wills Act
of Manitoba,9 the counterclaims sought by the Defendant which are predicated on her
entitlement to the administration of the estate of Andrew, have no merit at all, as the
Letters of Administration granted to her on the premise of Andrew’s intestacy, have
been found to have no basis. Thus, to continue her in the position of administratrix
under Letters erroneously issued in any event, would be inconsistent with the Will of
Andrew Clark executed by him in 2005 the formal validity of which has not been
challenged in these proceedings, and the continued validity of which has been upheld
in this judgment.
Disposition:
1. The Plaintiff’s claim therefore succeeds, in the following particulars:
i. A Declaration that the Plaintiff and the Deceased were in a common law
relationship under the law of Manitoba, Canada between August 1998 and
1 March 2016.
ii. A Declaration that the Will was valid and effectual as of the date of its
execution, and the Deceased died testate.
iii. A Declaration that the dispositive provisions of the Will remain in effect
and were not revoked by the Separation Agreement or otherwise.
iv. A Declaration that the Plaintiff is entitled to the beneficial interest in the
Deceased’s Estate.
v. An order that the Letters of Administration be revoked.
9 supra
78

vi. An order requiring the Defendant to account to the Plaintiff for all
dealings and transactions made by her, or on her behalf in the Deceased’s
Estate.
vii. Payment of all proceeds of such dealings and transactions on the taking
of the account.
viii. An injunction restraining the 2nd Defendant from disposing, charging,
otherwise dealing with any of the assets of the Deceased’s Estate whether
inside or outside the jurisdiction of this court.
2. The Defendant’s counterclaim is dismissed in its entirety.
3. The execution of this judgment as it relates to the operation of the company CG
Bytes, is stayed to abide further directions from the Manitoba Court.
4. Costs to be agreed, if not, to be taxed.

court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
